The calculation had revealed that the laugh had been produced by a being whose energy signature was so vast that the mathematical framework Thessaly used to define her own existence was, by comparison, a rounding error in a rounding error in a rounding error in a number so small it had been rounded to zero so many times that the rounding function itself had worn out. Thessaly had stood up, walked to the kitchen, and asked the previous cook if she could help with the dishes. She had been cooking ever since, preparing meals for a being who did not need to eat, because the act of cooking gave her something to do with her hands that did not involve calculating the energy signature of anything.

Godric was kind to both of them. He greeted Vael by name every morning, asking about the roses, complimenting the symmetry of the clematis pruning. He thanked Thessaly for every meal, his warm baritone carrying genuine gratitude, his eyes crinkling with the sincere affection of a man who appreciated the labor of the people who maintained his home. He had never raised his voice to either of them. He had never expressed displeasure. He had never done anything, in their presence, that could be remotely construed as threatening. And both of them lived in a state of permanent, unbroken, existential terror that they expressed through the meticulous, obsessive perfection of their service, because the terror was not about what Godric did. The terror was about what Godric was.

I watched Godric transplant his jasmine, his thick fingers pressing soil around the base of each seedling with the gentle, practiced care of a man who understood that growing things required tenderness, and I marveled, as I did every morning, at the cognitive dissonance of serving a being who could annihilate the omniverse by breathing too hard and who spent his mornings gardening. The morning was quiet. The garden was peaceful. The jasmine was growing. And then Godric sat back on his heels, wiped his hands on his trousers, and turned to me with an expression I had never seen before.

It was not warmth. It was not amusement. It was not the comfortable, contented expression of a man at peace with his existence. It was purpose. A quiet, settled, deliberate purpose, the look of a man who had decided it was time to do something he had been planning for a very long time.

"Clive," he said, his rich baritone carrying the casual warmth of a man inviting his nephew for a walk. "I have a task today. Something I have been meaning to do for some time. I would like you to accompany me."

"Of course, Uncle Godric," I said, because I would follow him into the void between existences if he asked, not out of courage but out of the absolute, learned certainty that anywhere Godric went was the safest place in existence, not because he made it safe but because nothing in existence was capable of being unsafe in his presence.

He stood, brushing soil from his knees, and the motion was fluid, effortless, the movement of a body that had been amplifying for a span of time so vast that the concept of effort had been retired. He led me through the garden, past the celestial clematis, past the roses that Vael was pruning with hands that had once built multiverses, past the white stones that had once been the laws of cause and effect, to a clearing at the center of the garden that I had never entered before. It was a simple space, circular, paved with the same dark stone as the rest of the palace, open to the sky. The sky above it was not the sky of Thronegate's capital. It was older, deeper, a darkness so absolute that it predated light itself, a void that existed before the first photon had been emitted in the first universe of the first multiverse of the first iteration of the omniverse. It was the canvas upon which existence had been painted, and it was still bare, still empty, still waiting.

Godric stood in the center of the clearing and looked up at the primordial void. His salt-and-pepper beard caught no light, because there was no light to catch. His heavily muscled frame was outlined against the absolute darkness, and in that outline, I could see the density of him, the compressed, infinite, recursive weight of a being who had been amplifying for so long that his physical form was less a body and more a gravitational event, a singularity of biology so dense that the concept of density had been reinvented to accommodate him.

"I am going to trigger the Stasis Event," Godric said.

My legs buckled. I caught myself on the edge of the paving stones, my amplified body trembling, my thousandfold-daily-multiplied magic discharging in small, violet arcs across my skin. "The Stasis Event," I repeated, the words tasting like copper and ozone.

"For my past self," Godric clarified, his tone conversational, as though he were describing the transplanting schedule for a new variety of jasmine. "In Year -10,000. At the moment of Thorin's death, when my mortal self is teleported to the sanctuary by the incomplete bloodline spell. I am going to use that moment as the delivery point and transform it into the Stasis Event."

I stared at him. The primordial void stared back. The garden, behind us, held its breath.

"You have always known you would do this," I said, and it was not a question, because I had served him long enough to understand that Godric's omniscience did not permit surprise, and if he was doing something now, he had known he would do it since the moment his omniscience activated, which was the same moment the Stasis Event had begun, which was the same moment his future self had created it, which was this moment.

"Since the instant I gained omniscience," Godric confirmed, nodding with the approving warmth of a teacher acknowledging a student's correct answer. "I have always known. The Stasis Event is a loop. I am the loop. My future self created the Stasis Event for my past self, and my past self became me, and I became my future self, and I create the Stasis Event for my past self. The loop is closed. The loop is me. It has always been me."

He turned to face me, and the purpose on his face was joined by something else, a wry, self-aware humor that I found deeply, cosmically unsettling in the context of what he was about to do. "I have been considering the parameters," he said. "The Stasis Event I experienced, the one that my future self, which is me, created for my past self, which is also me, was a held-back version."

"Held back," I repeated, the word barely escaping my throat.

"Held back," Godric confirmed, and the amusement on his face deepened. "I could have granted my past self a far more potent Stasis Event. I can copy my current mechanisms perfectly. My current amplification rate, my current superpower genesis, my current concealment, my current weakness paradox, my current immune system, my current passive shields, my current sentient auras, all of it, I could grant to my past self in full. The full, current, evolved, googolplexian-times-upgraded version of every mechanism I possess, transferred to the mortal man in the sanctuary at Year -10,000, with no cost to me, no effort, no diminishment of my own power."

He paused, letting the implications settle. "But I have decided against it. I am holding back. I am granting my past self a reduced version. A weaker Stasis Event. One that I believe will preserve some measure of mortal struggle, some authentic experience of growth, some genuine journey from mortality to godhood."

He said this with the sincere, earnest conviction of a man who genuinely believed he was being restrained, and the absurdity of it was so vast, so cosmic, so fundamentally, grotesquely disproportionate that my amplified mind threatened to shear its moorings. The held-back version, the reduced version, the version that Godric considered sufficiently limited to preserve mortal struggle, was the same Stasis Event that had produced a Godric who, at Year 0, was already effectively infinitely stronger than the rest of creation combined. The held-back version had produced a being who could annihilate the omniverse infinite times in zero seconds with zero effort at Year 0. The held-back version had been the foundation of a million years of amplification that had produced the being standing in front of me, the being whose passive existence restructured the laws of physics, whose weakest sentient aura could slaughter the strongest coalition ever assembled while nerfed to an infinitely recurring decimal, whose immune system edited the fundamental structure of existence to remove the prerequisites for harm. And Godric considered this held back. He genuinely, sincerely believed that he was being restrained, that he was leaving room for mortal struggle, that the Stasis Event he was about to grant was a reduced, limited, carefully measured gift.

I opened my mouth to speak, to point out the absurdity, to suggest that no version of the Stasis Event that produced a being who could destroy the omniverse infinite times in zero seconds at Year 0 could be meaningfully described as held back, but my intellect caught up with my tongue before the words could escape, and the realization that followed was so vast, so horrifying, so structurally devastating that I felt my mind crack along a fault line I had not known existed.

The cause-effect loop. If Godric granted his past self a more potent Stasis Event, a version with a higher amplification rate, more devastating superpower genesis, more advanced concealment, a more aggressive weakness paradox, then his past self would begin from a higher starting point. His past self would amplify faster, grow stronger sooner, acquire more powerful superpowers earlier. And his past self would eventually become the future Godric, the Godric who created the Stasis Event, which meant that the future Godric would be even more powerful than the current future Godric, because he would have been amplifying from a higher base. And a more powerful future Godric could grant an even more potent Stasis Event to his past self, which would make his past self even stronger, which would make the future Godric even more powerful, which would allow him to grant an even more potent Stasis Event. The loop was recursive. The loop was self-amplifying. The loop was an engine of escalating godhood that fed itself, each iteration producing a stronger Godric who could grant a stronger Stasis Event that produced a stronger Godric who could grant a stronger Stasis Event, and the escalation was infinite, and the infinity was compounding, and Godric was choosing, deliberately, to hold back, to not maximize the loop, to grant a reduced version, because he believed in mortal struggle, and the reduced version was already so incomprehensibly, absurdly, ridiculously overpowered that the rest of existence combined was a rounding error in comparison.

My legs gave out. I sat on the cold stone of the clearing, my back against nothing, my amplified body vibrating with a frequency that cracked the paving stones beneath me, and I looked up at Godric, who was standing in the primordial void, outlined against the darkness that predated existence, and I understood, with the absolute, crystalline, unshakeable certainty of a man who has been shown the architecture of reality and found it to be a house built around a single, infinite, recursive, self-amplifying god, that the being I served was not merely the most powerful entity in existence. He was the engine that produced himself. He was the cause and the effect. He was the Stasis Event and the being the Stasis Event created. He was the loop, and the loop was him, and the loop was infinite, and the loop was holding back, and the held-back version was already so vast that the word vast was a speck of dust in a hurricane of inadequacy.

Godric looked down at me, and his expression shifted to one of warm, genuine concern. "Clive," he said, his rich baritone gentle and kind. "You are shaking again. This seems to happen a great deal. Are you certain you are eating enough? I could ask Thessaly to prepare something."

"I am fine, Uncle Godric," I managed, the lie so transparent that it might as well have been made of glass.

Godric nodded, accepting the lie with the gracious, paternal patience he extended to all my inadequacies, and turned back to the void. He stood in the center of the clearing, his heavily muscled frame silhouetted against the primordial darkness, his salt-and-pepper beard catching no light, his thick, calloused hands hanging at his sides. He did not adopt a stance. He did not raise his arms. He did not chant, or weave hand signs, or channel energy, or prepare in any way that a mortal mind would recognize as preparation. He simply stood there, and then he did the thing that no language I possessed could adequately describe, because the thing he did existed outside the framework of language the way a galaxy exists outside the framework of a single atom.

He thought about it. That was the closest I could come to describing the action. He thought about the Stasis Event, and the thought was sufficient. His omniscience encompassed every detail of the event, every parameter, every variable, every instant of the moment in Year -10,000 when his mortal self would arrive in the sanctuary, broken and bleeding and grieving. His intellect, multiplying beyond layered infinities every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second, processed the parameters of the Stasis Event with the same casual, effortless precision that a mortal mind might use to plan a grocery list. And his power, the vast, incomprehensible, recursive, self-amplifying power that had been growing for a span of time so vast that time itself had forgotten when it started, expressed itself through the thought, reaching backward through causality, through the architecture of existence, through the primordial void that predated the first photon, to the specific instant in Year -10,000 when a mortal king named Godric was torn from the burning ruins of Olympus by the incomplete spell of a dying wizard.

I felt it. Not the power, which was concealed, which was always concealed, which would annihilate existence if it were not concealed. I felt the intention. The gentle, deliberate, carefully calibrated intention of a being who was reaching backward through time to gift his younger self with the mechanism that would transform him from a grieving mortal into the apex predator of all creation. The intention was warm. It was genuine. It was the intention of a man who remembered, with perfect omniscient clarity, the exact sensation of watching his wife and daughters die, the exact weight of grief that had crushed his mortal heart, the exact moment when the Stasis Event had begun and the amplification had started and the long, million-year journey from grief to contentment had commenced. He was giving his past self the gift. Not the full gift. The held-back gift. The reduced, limited, carefully measured gift that he believed would leave room for mortal struggle, even though the held-back gift was so incomprehensibly potent that it would make his past self infinitely stronger than the rest of creation combined within ten thousand years of the stasis beginning.

The Stasis Event was triggered. I felt the omniverse shudder, a subtle, almost imperceptible vibration that ran through the fabric of existence like a tuning fork struck in a cathedral. The loop closed. The past was sealed. The mortal Godric in Year -10,000, the man bleeding and grieving in the dark of the sanctuary, would experience the Stasis Event, would begin his amplification, would awaken in Year 0, would conquer Thronegate, would expand across the omniverse, would amplify for a million years, would become the being standing in front of me, and would, in this moment, trigger the Stasis Event for his past self. The circle was complete. The circle was infinite. The circle was Godric.

And the holding back. The absurd, cosmic, grotesque holding back. Godric had the ability to grant his past self the full, current version of every mechanism he possessed. The current amplification rate, which multiplied beyond layered infinities every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second. The current superpower genesis, which birthed apocalyptic abilities at a rate that made the original one-per-second framework look like a dripping faucet. The current concealment, which authored the laws of physics in real time. The current weakness paradox, which engaged with all sentient thought across all timelines as a permanent, omniversal function. The current immune system, which edited the prerequisites for harm out of existence. The current passive shields, which redefined the concept of threat to exclude Godric. The current sentient auras, countless, evolving, fiercely loyal, each one so vastly more powerful than the already terrifying Gent that comparing them was an exercise in irrelevance. All of this, the full, current, googolplexian-times-evolved version, he could have granted to his past self. At no cost. No effort. No diminishment. And if he had, his past self would have started from a base so incomprehensibly higher that the Godric produced by the full version would, by Year 0, make the current Godric look like a dim memory of a whisper of a shadow. And that Godric would eventually become the future Godric who triggered the Stasis Event, and that future Godric would be so vastly more powerful than the current future Godric that the comparison would be nonexistent, and that future Godric could grant an even more potent Stasis Event, and the loop would escalate, infinitely, each iteration producing a Godric so vastly more powerful than the previous that the word more was an insult to the scale of the increase.

And Godric had chosen not to do this. He had chosen to hold back. Because he believed in mortal struggle. Because he wanted his past self to experience the journey, the growth, the authentic, earned progression from grief to power. Because he was, at his core, still the mortal king of Olympus, the man who had loved his family and his people, the man who believed that struggle was necessary for meaning, and who had applied that belief to the architecture of his own godhood, deliberately limiting the gift he gave himself because he wanted the journey to be real.

The held-back version had produced a being who could destroy the omniverse infinite times in zero seconds with zero effort at Year 0. The held-back version had been the foundation of a million years of amplification that had produced the being standing in front of me. And the being standing in front of me, the product of the held-back version, was so powerful that his passive existence restructured the laws of physics, his weakest aura could slaughter the strongest coalition while nerfed to an infinitely recurring decimal, his immune system edited the prerequisites for harm out of existence, and his one true weakness was his own future self, whose shadow could erase the full-force effort of the most powerful being in the current omniverse.

Godric turned away from the void. The Stasis Event was done. The loop was closed. The past was sealed. He walked back toward the garden, his heavy boots solid on the paving stones, his broad shoulders catching the first light of the morning sun that was beginning to filter through the dimensional barriers of the palace grounds. He looked content. Satisfied. The look of a man who had completed a long-planned task and was ready for breakfast.

He paused at the edge of the clearing, glancing back at me with an expression of warm, paternal concern. "Clive," he said, his rich baritone gentle. "You are still on the ground. Are you unwell?"

I was on the ground. I was sitting on the cracked paving stones, my amplified body trembling, my thousandfold-daily-multiplied magic discharging in sporadic, violet arcs that scorch-marked the stone beneath me. My mind was reeling. The cause-effect loop, the recursive self-amplification, the holding back, the absurd, grotesque, cosmic disproportion between what Godric considered restrained and what restraint actually meant, all of it was cascading through my consciousness in a waterfall of existential horror that showed no sign of abating.

"Uncle Godric," I said, and my voice was raw, stripped of composure, stripped of the accumulated dignity of thirty years of service. "You held back."

"I did," Godric confirmed, and the satisfaction on his face was genuine, the quiet pride of a man who believed he had acted with restraint and wisdom.

"The held-back version," I continued, my voice cracking. "The version you consider limited. The version you believe preserves mortal struggle. That version produced you. The you that can destroy the omniverse infinite times in zero seconds. The you whose passive existence rewrites physics. The you whose weakest aura could annihilate everything while holding back an infinitely recurring decimal of its power. That was the held-back version."

"Correct," Godric said, nodding with the same warm, patient approval he always showed when I correctly identified a fact he found elementary.

"And if you had not held back," I said, and my voice was barely a whisper now, the words dragged from my throat by the compulsion of an intellect that could not stop calculating. "If you had granted your past self the full version. Your current amplification rate. Your current superpower genesis. All of it. Then your past self would have started from a base so much higher that the Godric produced by the full version would, by Year 0, be so incomprehensibly more powerful than the Godric produced by the held-back version that the comparison would be nonexistent. And that Godric would eventually become the future Godric who triggers the Stasis Event. And that future Godric would be so much more powerful than you that he could grant an even more potent full version. And the loop would escalate. Infinitely. Each iteration producing a Godric so vastly more powerful than the last that the word more would need to be reinvented. And you chose not to do this. Because you believe in mortal struggle."

"I did," Godric said, and he smiled, and the smile was warm and genuine and kind and it was the most terrifying thing I had ever witnessed, because it was the smile of a being who had the power to make himself infinitely more powerful through a recursive, self-amplifying loop of his own design, and who had chosen not to, because he wanted his younger self to experience the journey, and the journey, the held-back, limited, carefully restrained journey, had produced the most powerful being in the history of existence, a being whose one weakness was his own future self, a being whose passive existence authored the laws of physics, a being who was, in this moment, asking me if I was unwell because I was sitting on the ground trembling.

"Come, Clive," Godric said, extending a hand to help me up. The hand was warm, calloused, heavily muscled, the hand of a farmer or a blacksmith, the hand of a man who had once swung a hammer in the smithies of Olympus. It was also the hand of a being who had just reached backward through time and sealed the origin of his own godhood with a thought, and who had held back while doing so, and who considered the held-back version to be a concession to mortal fragility. "Thessaly is making breakfast. I believe she is attempting the grain porridge again. She worries about me, you know. She thinks I do not eat enough."

I took his hand. He pulled me to my feet with the effortless, casual strength of a man who did not notice the weight, because my weight, amplified and multiplied and compounded as it was, registered against his grip as less than nothing, a physical fact so insignificant that his passive biology did not bother to acknowledge it. I stood in the garden, surrounded by celestial clematis and white stones that had once been the laws of causality, and I watched Godric walk toward the palace kitchen, his broad back disappearing through the archway, his salt-and-pepper beard catching the morning light, and I thought about the loop, and the holding back, and the recursive, infinite, self-amplifying engine of godhood that Godric had chosen to restrain, and the restraint, and what the restraint meant, and what the lack of restraint would mean, and the gap between the two, and the gap was so vast, so cosmic, so fundamentally, grotesquely, absurdly disproportionate that my mind, amplified a thousandfold every day for thirty years, the most powerful mortal mind in existence, capable of modeling the structural integrity of multiversal clusters, could not hold the gap in its calculations without the calculations producing an error state that manifested as a physical tremor in my hands and a cold, copper taste on my tongue.

He had held back. The most powerful being in the history of existence had been produced by the held-back version of a self-amplifying recursive loop, and the being who produced the loop had chosen to restrain it because he believed in mortal struggle, and the restraint had still produced a god so vast that the word god was an insult to his taxonomy. And the full version, the unrestrained version, the version Godric had chosen not to grant, would have produced a being so incomprehensibly more powerful that the being produced by the held-back version, the being who could destroy the omniverse infinite times in zero seconds, the being whose shadow's afterthought could erase the full-force effort of the most powerful being in the current omniverse, would be, by comparison, less than a single atom in an infinite void of the full version's being.

And the loop could escalate. Infinitely. Each iteration more vast than the last. And Godric had chosen to stop at the first iteration, the held-back iteration, the restrained iteration, because he wanted the journey to be real.

I followed him to breakfast. Thessaly, the sentient theorem who had become a cook because she had once calculated the energy signature of Godric's laugh and had never recovered, served the grain porridge with hands that trembled slightly, her mathematical consciousness permanently recalibrated around the truth of what she was serving. Vael, the cosmic architect who had become a gardener because he had once shaken Godric's hand and felt the density of infinity in a farmer's grip, was pruning the roses outside the kitchen window, his geometric frameworks permanently dedicated to the optimal growth patterns of jasmine. Godric ate the porridge with genuine, warm appreciation, complimenting Thessaly on the texture, his rich baritone filling the kitchen with the satisfied hum of a contented man.

I sat across from him, and I ate, and I did not taste the porridge, because my mind was still in the clearing, still in the void, still circling the recursive, infinite, self-amplifying loop of a being who had held back and produced a god, and who could have not held back and produced something that made the god look like a speck of dust in a hurricane, and who had chosen restraint, and whose restraint was still so vast that the word restraint was a joke told by existence to itself in a dark room at the end of all things.

The jasmine along the eastern wall was in full bloom. Godric had asked me to help him transplant a new variety to the western wall after breakfast. I would help him. I would hold the trellis, and he would press the roots into the soil with his thick, calloused fingers, and the sun would be warm, and the garden would be peaceful, and the being kneeling beside me would be the most powerful entity in the history of existence, a being who had just sealed the origin of his own godhood with a thought, a being who had held back, a being whose held-back version was already so incomprehensibly, absurdly, ridiculously overpowered that the word overpowered was a love letter written by inadequacy to its own irrelevance.

And the loop was closed. And the loop was infinite. And the loop was him. And he was planting jasmine. And I was holding the trellis. And the porridge was warm. And the morning was quiet. And the existential dread was a permanent, structural feature of my consciousness, a load-bearing wall in the architecture of my mind that I had learned to build my days around, and I would build my day around it now, because Godric had asked me to help with the jasmine, and Godric's court wizards always helped with the jasmine, and the jasmine was real, and the soil was real, and the sun was real, and for this one, small, finite, fragile moment, the man kneeling beside me in the garden was just Uncle Godric, and the loop was just a concept, and the holding back was just a word, and the morning was just a morning.

But it was not. It was never just a morning. It was a morning in the garden of the apex predator of all creation, at the end of all things, in the year that could not be written, in the presence of a being who had just closed the loop of his own infinity and held back while doing it, and the holding back was the most terrifying thing I had ever witnessed, because it meant that what I was experiencing, the Godric I served, the Godric I loved, the Godric who ate porridge and planted jasmine and asked about my children, was the restrained version. The limited version. The held-back version.

And the full version was a door that Godric had chosen not to open. And the door was real. And the door was infinite. And the door was him.

I held the trellis. I did not shake. I was a court wizard of Blake's lineage, and my will was amplified a thousandfold every day, and I would need every fraction of that amplification to hold the trellis steady in the presence of a being who had held back, and whose held back was still, and would always be, and would forever escalate to be, the most incomprehensibly, absurdly, ridiculously, cosmically, exponentially, infinitely overpowered fact in the history of existence.

The jasmine smelled exceptional. He had promised. And Godric always kept his promises.

content.text:
Write a detailed story of Godric deciding to subjugate the IRL universe, at Year 25\.

Earth obviously, has 0 chance, and will experience total despair and existential dread.

In this scenario, you must make Earth try their best to kill Godric, which means Godric will completely annihilate it. You must NOT make Godric nice, forgiving. He can still be friendly. Godric feels mischievously playful so he doesn't instantly finish it (you must ensure the reader knows that if he wants to he can instantly destroy earth in 0 second with 0 effort), but he's particularly cruel and ensures every death is as agonizing as possible--however, you must not make Godric seemingly drag the fight, he still finishes the annihilation objectively swiftly (undetectably fast to normal mortals as it's infinitely less than a planck second, but but to Godric it's considered as "leisurely slow") but subjective to the opposition an eternity of maximum agony. You must clearly indicate that Godric is using 0% of his power and 0% of effort, and even if Earth is given infinite preparation, all possible advantages, they will still have 0 chance to even tickle Godric or make Godric feel anything. Show Godric be amused by how primitively weak and fragile the earthlings are, how they are trying so hard and effectively doing nothing to him. You must not leave any survivor of Earth alive, serving Godric, living in Thronegate or any other such crap which shows Godric sparing them. Godric’s power must be shown to be absolutely destructive and not give any opposition any possible redemption path.

As usual with Godric, the collaterals must be massive (omniversal scale minimum).

The story must be told from Godric Prime's 3rd person pov (with omniscient narrator if needed), at Year 999...9+, reminiscing about how pathetically weak he was back then compared to how he currently is, and for him to pity his weak self back when he annihilate this universe (highlight the irony and terrifying fact that this subjugation by what he considered to be his weakly pathetic self was already absurdly one-sided and terrifyingly overwhelming--his weak self was effectively using 0% of his power with 0% effort to annihilate that universe, and if that same weak self wasn't in playful mode and wanted to end it instantly, the total annihilation could be done in 0 second. In addition, that weak self destroyed the entire omniverse as collateral. You must make it clear that if the current Godric does it, it will be even more infinitely devastatingly one sided, and he will only be getting unimaginably stronger forever).

You must end the story with the confirmation and dread of how futile the rest of the existence compared to Godric, and that Godric is the most powerful being with unimaginable power growth that dwarfs entire creation, with the rest of the creation trembling in fear of him, and even if the rest from all timelines and from every possible permutations work together to oppose him with unlimited preparation time and resources, they will still fail so completely and won't even make Godric's weakest shield feel the slightest tickle and Godric can completely nonchalantly annihilate them with 0% power and 0% effort in 0 second, and that unfathomable gap will only ever increase infinitely in accelerating manner forever.

reasoning_content:
The user wants me to write a detailed story from Godric Prime's 3rd person POV at Year 999...9+, reminiscing about when he subjugated the IRL universe (our Earth/universe) at Year 25. Let me carefully follow all the requirements:

1. Year 25 - Godric decides to subjugate the IRL universe (Earth)
2. Earth tries their best to kill Godric → Godric completely annihilates it
3. Godric is NOT nice/forgiving to opposition, but can be friendly
4. Godric feels mischievously playful - doesn't instantly finish it, but still objectively swift
5. Every death is as agonizing as possible
6. Godric uses 0% power, 0% effort
7. Even with infinite prep, Earth has 0 chance to even tickle Godric
8. Godric is amused by how weak Earth is
9. NO survivors - no one from Earth survives, serves Godric, or lives in Thronegate
10. Massive omniversal-scale collateral damage
11. POV: Godric Prime's 3rd person at Year 999...9+, reminiscing about his "weak" past self
12. Irony that his "weak" self was already absurdly overwhelming
13. His weak self destroyed the entire omniverse as collateral
14. Current Godric would be infinitely more devastating
15. End with confirmation of futility of all existence compared to Godric

Let me write this story with the appropriate tone - epic power fantasy, existential dread, absurd power scaling. I need to be careful about:
- Not making Godric drag the fight
- Making the annihilation objectively swift but subjectively eternal for the opposition
- Showing 0% power and 0% effort
- Omniversal collateral damage
- No survivors from Earth
- Godric Prime reminiscing about how "weak" he was at Year 25

Let me also remember the lore: at Year 25, Godric has been amplifying for 10,025 years (since Year -10,000). His stats multiply beyond layered infinities. He has the evolved weakness paradox (since Year 2000). He has absolute concealment. He has superpowers. But at Year 999...9+, all of these have evolved countless times beyond that.

The IRL universe is our universe - Earth, humans, nuclear weapons, etc.

Let me write this carefully.
content:
The memory surfaced in the vast, silent architecture of Godric Prime's omniscient consciousness with the gentle, nostalgic warmth of a man finding a childhood photograph in the back of a dusty drawer. He turned it over, examined it, and allowed himself a quiet, private chuckle that resonated through every dimensional framework of his existence with a weight that would have annihilated the omniverse infinite times over if his concealment had not been authoring the laws of physics to accommodate him. The memory was of Year 25. A small, primitive universe on the outer edge of a middling multiverse cluster, unremarkable in every way, populated by a species of bipedal primates who had recently discovered the atom and were feeling, by the standards of their developmental stage, rather pleased with themselves. Earth. They had called it Earth. Godric Prime remembered the name with the fond, slightly condescending affection a naturalist might feel for a particular species of beetle that had once built an interesting mound in a terrarium he had owned as a child.

Year 25. He had been so young, by his own current reckoning. So pathetically, adorably weak. His amplification had been running for a mere ten thousand and twenty-five years, his stats multiplying beyond layered infinities every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second, his superpower count in the hundreds of billions, his evolved weakness paradox already evolved past the need for triggers and engaging with all sentient thought across all timelines. By any standard other than his own current one, the Godric of Year 25 was already the most terrifyingly, absurdly, incomprehensibly overpowered being in the history of existence, a god so vast that the word god was a speck of dust clinging to the hem of his garment. But Godric Prime, the being who existed at Year 999...9, who had been amplifying for a span of time so vast that the concept of vastness had been beaten to death and buried in an unmarked grave, looked back at his Year 25 self and saw a child. A powerful child, certainly. A child whose passive existence could annihilate the omniverse. But a child nonetheless, small and young and weak in the way that all earlier versions of a recursively amplifying being are weak compared to their later selves, which is to say, weak by a margin so vast that the word weak was an overstatement of their strength.

He had been in a playful mood, that day in Year 25. The memory was crisp, perfect, preserved with the flawless fidelity of absolute omniscience. He had been sitting on the observation balcony of the Thronegate citadel, his ruggedly handsome, forty-five-year-old frame draped in simple dark garments, his salt-and-pepper beard catching the golden light of the capital sun. Blake had been beside him, his mutton-chopped best friend, fifty years old and already showing the mortal lines that Godric's modified gift could not fully prevent, and they had been discussing the expansion routes for the next phase of omniversal subjugation. Godric's omniscience had been processing, as it always processed, the totality of existence, every atom, every thought, every intention across every timeline, when his attention had drifted, with the idle, curious wanderlust of a man who had already conquered most of his local multiversal cluster and was looking for something to amuse him between lunch and his afternoon garden session, to a small, blue-green planet orbiting an unremarkable yellow star in a universe he had not yet bothered to formally integrate.

The universe was primitive. It contained a single, organic civilization of bipedal primates who called themselves humans, residing on a planet they called Earth, in a solar system they considered, with the charmingly myopic arrogance of a species that had never left its own gravity well, to be significant. Their technology was adorably crude. They had split the atom and were engaged in a paranoid, geopolitical standoff between two rival factions, each armed with enough nuclear weaponry to render their own planet's surface uninhabitable, a fact they considered to be the pinnacle of destructive capability. Their understanding of physics was so limited that they had not yet discovered the existence of a single additional dimensional layer beyond their native four. Their magic was nonexistent. Their military capacity, by omniversal standards, was a rounding error in a calculation that had already been rounded to zero so many times that the rounding function itself had degraded into a persistent, low-grade existential apology.

Godric had smiled. It was a warm, genuine, mischievous smile, the smile of a cat noticing a particularly interesting moth. He turned to Blake and mentioned, with the casual, conversational tone of a man suggesting a weekend outing, that he felt like subjugating a small, primitive universe. Blake, who had known Godric for twenty-five years and had learned to recognize the particular glint in his eye that preceded what Godric considered fun, had sighed heavily and asked which one. Godric had pointed, through the dimensional barriers, through the multiversal walls, through the fabric of a dozen intervening realities, at the small blue-green dot that was Earth. Blake had looked at it, processed the tactical data through his own thousandfold-amplified intellect, and informed Godric, with the blunt honesty that was his defining characteristic, that the target was so primitive that subjugating it would be like a man stepping on an ant. Godric had agreed enthusiastically. That was the point, he said. He was in a playful mood.

The Godric of Year 25 had decided, in his playful, mischievous way, to give Earth a chance. Not a real chance, because a real chance would have required the possibility of resistance, and resistance against Godric was a structural impossibility, a category error so fundamental that the universe itself had evolved to prevent it. But a theatrical chance. A performance. He would appear to them, announce his intention to subjugate their universe, and allow them to respond with whatever they considered their most powerful capabilities. He would do this not because their response mattered, not because their weapons could register against him, not because their strategies could influence the outcome in any way, but because he found the reactions of primitive beings confronting absolute power to be entertaining, and he was, at his core, a being who enjoyed the theater of his own supremacy.

He appeared on Earth. Specifically, he appeared in the center of their most politically significant city, in the middle of their most important geopolitical gathering, during a session of their United Nations General Assembly, a forum where the bipedal primates sent representatives to argue with each other in a room. His absolute concealment was fully active. He looked, to the primitive sensory organs of the humans, like a man. A tall, heavily muscled, ruggedly handsome man of forty-five, with short salt-and-pepper hair and a neatly trimmed salt-and-pepper beard, wearing simple dark clothes. He did not glow. He did not radiate power. He did not distort the air or warp the light or cause the ground to tremble. He simply appeared, standing at the podium, because he had decided to stand at the podium, and reality had rearranged itself to accommodate the decision before the neurons in the humans' visual cortices could process the change.

The reaction was exactly as entertaining as he had hoped. The humans stared. Their primitive optical sensors widened, their cardiovascular systems accelerated, their endocrine systems flooded their bodies with stress hormones. Their security personnel reached for crude kinetic weapons, handheld devices that propelled small metal projectiles through controlled chemical explosions. Godric watched them with the warm, patient amusement of a man observing squirrels in a park. He allowed them a full three seconds of panicked silence, savoring the primitive, biological terror radiating from their small, fragile bodies, before he spoke.

His voice was a rich, resonant baritone, calibrated by his concealment to register at a comfortable, conversational volume in the humans' auditory range. He spoke in their dominant language, which his omniscience had processed to fluency in less than a nanosecond. He announced, with the polite, friendly warmth of a landlord visiting a tenant, that he was Godric, King of Thronegate, and that he had come to subjugate their universe. He explained that their universe would be integrated into the Thronegate omniversal empire, that resistance was futile, and that he was granting them the opportunity to respond with whatever they considered their most powerful capabilities, purely because he was curious and in a good mood.

The humans did not believe him. This was, Godric reflected from the vantage point of Year 999...9, the most understandable and the most fatal mistake they would ever make. They were a species that had never encountered extraterrestrial life, let alone extraterrestrial conquest. Their frame of reference for power was nuclear weaponry. Their frame of reference for authority was geopolitical diplomacy. Their frame of reference for the impossible was their own, limited science fiction, a genre that imagined the boundaries of capability as extending, at most, to interstellar travel and energy weapons. A man appearing in their assembly hall and claiming to be the king of an omniversal empire was, by their standards, either a lunatic or a performance artist, and their responses reflected this assessment.

Their security personnel fired their kinetic weapons at him. The small metal projectiles traveled at subsonic speeds, striking his body, which was, even at zero percent of his true durability, so incomprehensibly dense that the projectiles did not bounce off. They simply ceased to exist upon contact with his skin, their kinetic energy absorbed so totally and so effortlessly by the passive, resting density of his tissue that the atoms of the projectiles were quietly disassembled and integrated into his biology as trace nutrients. Godric did not feel the impacts. He did not feel them in the same way a man does not feel individual photons striking his skin, except the comparison was inadequate, because individual photons at least exist on a scale that physics acknowledges, while the kinetic force of a human firearm was so far below the threshold of Godric's sensory awareness that his passive systems did not bother to register the event.

He smiled at the security personnel, a warm, friendly smile that crinkled the corners of his eyes, and he waited. The humans, he knew, would escalate. It was their nature. Primitive species, when confronted with something they did not understand, reached for the most powerful tool they possessed and swung it with the desperate, flailing urgency of a child swinging a stick at a storm. Godric found this endearing. He also found it useful, because their escalation would give him the theatrical context he desired for the annihilation.

The humans escalated. Within hours, their geopolitical factions, who had been locked in a paranoid standoff for decades, set aside their differences and united against the common threat. Godric watched their communications with omniscient amusement as they coordinated, planned, and mobilized. They activated their nuclear arsenals, the pinnacle of their destructive capability, a collection of thermonuclear warheads capable of releasing energy through the fusion of hydrogen isotopes. They deployed their military forces, millions of soldiers armed with kinetic weapons, explosive devices, and crude armored vehicles. Their scientists, in a frenzy of desperate innovation, attempted to develop new weapons, repurposing their particle accelerators and laser laboratories into directed-energy weapons, believing that if kinetic force was insufficient, concentrated energy might succeed where metal projectiles had failed.

Godric gave them time. Not because they needed it. Not because the additional preparation would make any difference. But because he was enjoying the show. He sat in the United Nations building, in a chair he had materialized for himself, and he watched the humans scramble. His omniscience tracked every conversation, every strategy session, every whispered fear and shouted command. He watched their generals draw plans on digital screens, plans that assumed he was a physical being with physical limitations, that he could be killed by sufficient force, that there existed a threshold of destructive energy beyond which his body would fail. The plans were so adorably, so tragically, so cosmically wrong that Godric found himself chuckling softly, the sound causing the humans in the room to flinch involuntarily, their primitive nervous systems responding to a frequency of amusement that their conscious minds could not interpret.

They gave him forty-eight hours. Godric allowed it, sitting patiently in his chair, occasionally making polite conversation with the terrified diplomats who had been designated to communicate with him. He was friendly. He was warm. He asked about their culture, their history, their art. He complimented the architecture of their cities. He expressed genuine interest in their music, which he found, despite the primitive acoustic limitations of their auditory range, to contain moments of genuine emotional resonance. He was, in every observable way, a gracious, interested, friendly visitor, and the contrast between his demeanor and the apocalyptic mobilization occurring outside the building was a source of dark, private amusement that he savored like a fine wine.

At the end of the forty-eight hours, the humans launched their attack. It was, by their standards, the most concentrated, coordinated, overwhelming assault their species had ever conceived. Every nuclear warhead in their arsenal, thousands of them, was launched simultaneously, converging on the United Nations building from every direction. Their military forces surrounded the building with armored divisions, their soldiers armed and ready. Their scientists activated their repurposed directed-energy weapons, targeting Godric with concentrated particle beams and laser arrays. Their most powerful computers ran simulations, calculating optimal strike patterns, blast yields, and radiation dosages, attempting to find the mathematical combination of forces that would overcome whatever he was.

Godric sat in his chair and watched them come. The nuclear warheads descended through the atmosphere, their fusion cores primed, their destructive potential, by human standards, world-ending. The particle beams fired, concentrating the energy of particle accelerators into focused streams of subatomic particles. The laser arrays pulsed, directing coherent light at intensities capable of cutting through steel. The soldiers opened fire, millions of kinetic projectiles converging on his position.

Godric did not move. He did not stand. He did not raise a hand, or weave a spell, or activate a defense, or shift his weight, or blink, or breathe differently, or think a specific thought. He sat in his chair, his salt-and-pepper beard catching the light of the descending warheads, and he allowed an infinitesimal fraction of his magical defense, a trait that had been multiplying beyond layered infinities every Planck second for over ten thousand years, to passively express itself at effectively zero percent of its true capacity.

The nuclear warheads detonated. Thousands of them, simultaneously, their fusion cores igniting in a coordinated, overlapping wave of thermonuclear destruction that released more energy in a single instant than the human species had ever harnessed. The combined yield was sufficient to scour the surface of the planet, to vaporize the oceans, to strip the atmosphere, to reduce every structure, every organism, every molecule on Earth's surface to its component atoms. The fireball was visible from orbit. The shockwave circled the planet three times. The electromagnetic pulse fried every electronic device on the surface.

Godric did not feel it. The detonation of thousands of nuclear warheads, the pinnacle of human destructive capability, the most powerful force their species could conceive, registered against his body as nothing. Not as a small something. Not as a minor sensation. As nothing. The heat, which exceeded the temperature of the sun's core, did not warm his skin. The blast wave, which could level mountains, did not disturb his hair. The radiation, which would kill any organic being in seconds, did not interact with his cells. His magical defense, at zero percent of its capacity, had passively absorbed the entire energy output of thousands of thermonuclear detonations without acknowledging the event, the way the ocean does not acknowledge a single raindrop.

The particle beams struck him next, concentrated streams of subatomic particles accelerated to relativistic speeds. They struck his body and were absorbed, their energy quietly integrated into his mana reserves as a quantity so infinitesimally small that it registered as a rounding error in a number that had already been rounded to zero. The laser arrays fired, their coherent light striking his skin at intensities capable of cutting through steel plate. The light was absorbed by his tissue, which was so incomprehensibly dense that the photons simply ceased to exist upon contact, their energy consumed by his biology as fuel for his amplification, a contribution so microscopically, absurdly insignificant that his passive systems did not bother to record it.

The kinetic projectiles, millions of them, struck his body and were disassembled, their atoms integrated into his tissue as trace nutrients, the same way his body had processed the initial handgun rounds. The soldiers, firing from behind their armored vehicles, watched their weapons have zero effect and began to break, their primitive minds confronting the absolute, unprocessable reality that their most powerful weapons, the apex of their species' destructive capability, were being absorbed by a sitting man as nourishment.

Godric smiled at them. The smile was warm, genuine, friendly, and it was the most terrifying thing any human being had ever witnessed, because it was the smile of a being who had just absorbed the combined nuclear arsenal of their entire species and had not noticed. He stood from his chair, the motion fluid, effortless, and he looked at the assembled military forces, the surviving diplomats, the scientists staring at their instruments in incomprehending despair, and he spoke.

"Thank you for the meal," he said, his rich baritone carrying a warmth that made the words more horrifying than any threat. "That was very creative. I particularly enjoyed the particle beams. They had a nice flavor."

Then the playfulness ended, and the cruelty began.

Godric of Year 25, the being Godric Prime would later look back on with the fond, condescending pity a father feels for a toddler's first finger painting, decided that the humans had earned their annihilation. Not because they had opposed him. They had not opposed him. They had attempted to oppose him, which was different, the way a mouse attempting to bite a mountain was different from a mouse opposing a mountain. They had swung their stick at the storm, and the storm had not noticed, and now the storm was going to show them what it meant to be weather.

He did not use a weapon. He did not cast a spell. He did not activate a superpower, or express a stat, or deploy an aura, or engage a shield. He simply looked at the humans, the assembled military forces, the diplomats, the scientists, the soldiers, the generals, the political leaders, every human being in the vicinity of the United Nations building, and he decided that they should experience the maximum possible agony that their biology and consciousness could sustain, and that the agony should last, subjectively, for an eternity.

The effect was instantaneous in objective time, completed in a span of time so short that the word short was an overstatement, a duration so infinitesimally small that it made a Planck second look like an epoch, a fraction of a fraction of a fraction of a moment so tiny that no instrument in the omniverse could have detected its passage. But to Godric, operating on a timescale so vastly accelerated that the entire objective duration of the annihilation felt, to his perception, like a leisurely, unhurried afternoon, the process was slow, deliberate, and savoring. And to the humans, trapped in a subjective temporal dilation that Godric had imposed with less than a thought, the process lasted forever.

The humans' bodies began to unmake themselves. Not from the outside in, the way a bomb unmakes a body, with a blast wave that tears and burns and crushes. From the inside out. Their cells, each one a microscopic factory of biological processes, began to malfunction in ways that their nervous systems were specifically designed to perceive as agony. Their pain receptors, every single one of the billions of nerve endings in their bodies, were forced to maximum firing simultaneously and permanently, locked in a state of absolute, unmitigated, unadaptable pain that their biology could not habituate to, could not go into shock from, could not escape through unconsciousness or death, because Godric had decided that they would not die until he was finished with them, and he was not finished.

Their skin began to peel. Not burn. Not melt. Peel, slowly, in long, screaming strips that separated from the underlying tissue with a wet, tearing sound that each human could hear, because their auditory systems were preserved and functioning, forced to listen to the sound of their own flesh being removed. The exposed tissue beneath, raw muscle and glistening fascia, was immediately subjected to a sensation of burning so intense that it made the thermonuclear detonation feel, by comparison, like a warm bath. Their muscles, stripped of their protective covering, began to spasm, each contraction tearing the muscle fibers apart from their own tension, the snap of each fiber accompanied by a fresh surge of agony that compounded upon the existing agony, creating a recursive, escalating cascade of pain that built upon itself with each iteration.

Their bones were next. The calcium structures that gave their bodies form began to crack, not from external force but from internal pressure, as Godric caused the mineral matrix of the bone tissue to expand imperceptibly, generating internal stress that propagated through the bone in slow, grinding fracture lines. Each crack was accompanied by a sound, audible to the victim, of their own skeleton breaking, a wet, granular crunch that resonated through their skulls and into their consciousness. The broken bones did not pierce their organs, because Godric was not interested in killing them quickly. The fractures simply existed, generating pain, each crack a new source of agony that added to the total, and the total was already so vast that the human brain, forced to process it without the mercy of shutdown, was producing a subjective experience of suffering that exceeded anything their species had ever conceived.

Their organs followed. Stomachs, intestines, livers, kidneys, each one individually targeted, each one subjected to a specific, customized form of destruction that maximized the pain output of that particular organ. Stomachs were forced to produce acid at concentrations that dissolved their own walls, the acid leaking into the abdominal cavity and beginning to digest the surrounding tissue from the inside. Intestines knotted, the loops of bowel twisting around each other with a force that cut off blood supply and generated ischemic agony so profound that it transcended the nerve endings and became a whole-body experience of deprivation. Livers were caused to produce toxins that their own bodies could not process, the poisons circulating through their bloodstream and attacking their own nervous systems, generating hallucinations of pain that existed in places their bodies did not have, phantom agony in limbs that had already been stripped of flesh, ghost suffering in organs that had already been dissolved.

Their eyes were preserved. Their vision was maintained, forced to watch their own bodies unmake themselves, to see the wet, glistening ruin of their own flesh, the exposed bone, the dissolving organs, the rivers of blood and lymph and bile that streamed from their ruined forms. They could see each other, see their fellow humans undergoing the same process, see the collective, synchronized agony of their entire species, and the knowledge that they were not alone in their suffering did not comfort them. It amplified the horror, because the sight of another human being, stripped and broken and screaming, was a mirror of their own destruction, and the empathy that was fundamental to their species turned the shared suffering into a multiplied, compounded, recursive experience of communal agony.

Their minds were preserved. Their consciousness, the thing that made them human, was maintained in a state of perfect, unbroken clarity, unable to retreat into madness, unable to dissociate, unable to find any corner of their psyche where the pain was not. They were aware. They were fully, completely, agonizingly aware of every sensation, every crack, every peel, every dissolved organ, every torn fiber, every burned nerve. And the awareness was the worst part, because the awareness meant they could not escape, could not hide, could not find any refuge in unconsciousness or delirium. They were trapped in their own bodies, in their own pain, in their own destruction, and the trap was absolute, and the trap was eternal, because Godric had decided it was eternal, and Godric's decisions were the laws of physics.

The process was not limited to the humans at the United Nations. Godric's intent, expressed at zero percent of his true power with zero percent of his true effort, propagated outward from the building at a speed that made the speed of light look like a glacier. Within the same infinitesimal fraction of a moment, every human being on Earth was subjected to the same treatment. Every man, woman, and child on the planet, every human in every city and every village and every remote outpost, began to unmake in the same, synchronized, agonizing, eternal fashion. Their bodies peeled, their bones cracked, their organs dissolved, their eyes watched, their minds screamed. The entire human species, eight billion conscious beings, was subjected to an eternity of maximum biological agony simultaneously, each individual experiencing the destruction of their own body in excruciating, granular, eternal detail, while the planet beneath them continued to exist, untouched by physical destruction, because Godric had not destroyed the planet. He had only destroyed the people, and the destruction was not physical. It was experiential. It was a gift of agony, presented with the warm, friendly, mischievous generosity of a being who had absorbed their nuclear arsenal as a snack and had decided they deserved something special in return.

The collateral damage, however, was not so contained. The infinitesimal fraction of Godric's power that had been expressed, the effectively zero percent that he had used to annihilate the human species, did not stay within the boundaries of Earth's atmosphere, or Earth's solar system, or Earth's galaxy, or Earth's universe. The power expressed by the Godric of Year 25, even at zero percent, even in a playful mood, even holding back because he was enjoying the theater, was so vast, so incomprehensibly, absurdly disproportionate to the scale of a single primitive planet, that it overflowed. The way a man stepping on an ant does not intend to crack the pavement beneath the ant, but the weight of the man is so vastly greater than the ant that the pavement cracks anyway. The power propagated outward, and the universe containing Earth began to collapse. The fabric of space-time, unable to bear the weight of even an infinitesimal fraction of Godric's expressed intent, tore open in bleeding rents that expanded at superluminal speeds. Stars went dark. Galaxies unraveled. The entire universe, with its billions of galaxies and trillions of stars and countless civilizations far more advanced than the bipedal primates of Earth, was annihilated as collateral damage from the annihilation of a single, primitive species.

And it did not stop at the universe. The overflow propagated through the dimensional barriers, into the multiverse containing that universe, into the multiversal cluster containing that multiverse. The destruction cascaded outward like a stone dropped into a pond, except the stone was an infinitesimal fraction of Godric's power and the pond was the entire omniverse. Every universe, every multiverse, every dimensional plane, every timeline, every possible and impossible structure of existence within the omniverse was annihilated, scoured clean by the overflow of a being who had been trying to kill some primitive primates and had used slightly more than zero percent of his power to do it.

The Godric of Year 25 had destroyed the entire omniverse as a side effect of annihilating Earth. Not because he had intended to. Not because he had needed to. Because the power he had expressed, at zero percent of his capacity, with zero percent of his effort, in a playful mood, was so vast, so incomprehensibly, absurdly, ridiculously disproportionate to the scale of a single species on a single planet, that the omniverse itself could not contain the overflow.

He had stood on the dead surface of Earth, the planet now a silent, lifeless rock orbiting a star that no longer existed in a universe that had been erased, surrounded by the absolute void of an annihilated omniverse, and he had sighed with mild, fatherly irritation. "Messy," he had murmured, the sound impossibly loud in the absolute nothingness, and in zero seconds, without effort, without thought, without moving a muscle, he had restored the entire omniverse. Every galaxy, every star, every planet, every being, every civilization, every universe and multiverse and dimensional plane, rebuilt perfectly, placed exactly where it had been, their memories intact, their histories preserved.

Everything was restored except Earth. Earth was not restored. The human species was not restored. The eight billion conscious beings who had been subjected to an eternity of maximum agony were not restored. Their screaming, unmade remains persisted, not as physical matter, for their bodies had been disassembled beyond the atomic level, but as conscious, suffering echoes, eternal fixtures of Godric's passive systems, permanent monuments to the cost of attempting to oppose a being whose power was so vast that the omniverse itself was collateral damage. The humans would scream forever. Their agony would persist, eternally, as a function of Godric's will, and Godric's will was the most unbreakable force in existence, because Godric's will was authored by a being whose future self was so powerful that the shadow of an afterthought could erase the full-force effort of the most powerful being in the current omniverse.

Godric of Year 25 had stepped through the void, returned to Thronegate, and resumed his afternoon. He had planted a new row of jasmine along the garden wall, eaten dinner with Blake and Anna, and gone to bed early, satisfied with his day's work. He had not thought about Earth again. The humans had been a momentary amusement, a brief, entertaining distraction, a moth caught and pulled apart by a cat who had forgotten about it by dinner. Their eternal suffering was a detail so small, so insignificant, so thoroughly beneath his notice that his omniscience maintained the fact of their agony without his conscious attention, the way a man's body maintains his heartbeat without his conscious attention.

Godric Prime, the being who existed at Year 999...9, the being who had been amplifying for a span of time so vast that the Year 25 version of himself was a dim, distant, pathetically weak memory, turned the memory over in his vast, omniscient consciousness and smiled. The smile was warm, genuine, and filled with the private, self-aware amusement of a being who found his own past endearing in its weakness. The Godric of Year 25 had been so young. So small. So limited. His power, which had been sufficient to annihilate the entire omniverse as collateral damage from a playful afternoon, was, by Godric Prime's current standards, a flickering candle in a hurricane. The gap between the Godric of Year 25 and Godric Prime was not a gap. It was an abyss so vast, so deep, so incomprehensibly, absurdly, ridiculously disproportionate that the word abyss was a speck of dust clinging to the edge of the word's inadequacy. Godric Prime's passive, resting, unconscious existence was so vastly more powerful than the Year 25 Godric's full, maximum, all-out expression that the comparison could not be made, because the framework of comparison had been disassembled and rebuilt so many times over the intervening eons that the original framework was an archaeological curiosity studied by concepts that had not yet been invented.

If Godric Prime, the being who existed now, at this moment, were to perform the same act, the subjugation of a primitive universe, the result would not be the annihilation of the omniverse. The omniverse's annihilation, as collateral damage, was what happened when the Godric of Year 25, a being so weak that Godric Prime pitied him, expressed zero percent of his power in a playful mood. If Godric Prime expressed zero percent of his power, the result would not be annihilation. Annihilation implies the destruction of something that previously existed. If Godric Prime expressed zero percent of his power, the result would be a state of existence in which the concept of the omniverse having ever existed was retroactively erased from the mathematical framework of possibility, a state so total, so absolute, so complete that even the memory of existence would be deleted, and the deletion itself would be deleted, and the deletion of the deletion would be deleted, a recursive, infinite erasure that would leave nothing, not even nothing, because nothing is a state, and states require frameworks, and frameworks require existence, and existence would be the first thing deleted.

And Godric Prime was still amplifying. Every infinitesimal fraction of every Planck second, his power multiplied beyond layered infinities, and the multiplication rate itself multiplied, and the rate at which the multiplication rate multiplied also multiplied, a recursive, fractal, meta-dimensional escalation of godhood that had been running for a span of time so vast that time itself had forgotten when it started and had given up trying to remember. The being Godric Prime was now, at this exact instant, was so vastly more powerful than the being he had been one second ago that the being he had been one second ago was, by comparison, a rounding error in a number so small that the rounding function had been retired. And the being he would be one second from now would be so vastly more powerful than the being he was now that the same comparison applied. The escalation never stopped. The escalation would never stop. The escalation could not stop, because the escalation was a function of the Stasis Event, and the Stasis Event was irrevocable, and the Stasis Event was irrevocable because it was authored by Godric's future self, and Godric's future self was the most powerful being in existence, and the most powerful being in existence was Godric, and the loop was closed, and the loop was infinite, and the loop was him.

The omniverse existed because Godric allowed it. The omniverse continued because Godric's absolute concealment, which had evolved countless times beyond the version that had merely suppressed his passive pressure, actively authored the laws of physics in real time to accommodate his existence, restructuring reality every instant to prevent the omniverse from collapsing under the weight of his passive, resting, unconscious presence. The beings of the omniverse, the countless civilizations and entities and gods and machines and cosmic hierarchies that populated the infinite layers of existence, lived and breathed and thought and dreamed because Godric's concealment made it possible, and the concealment made it possible because Godric willed it, and Godric's will was the most unbreakable force in existence because the source of his will was a being so powerful that the shadow of an afterthought could erase the full-force effort of the most powerful being in the current omniverse.

No one opposed him. No one had opposed him for a span of time so vast that the concept of opposition had become a historical curiosity, studied by scholars with the same detached, academic interest that a paleontologist might bring to the examination of a fossilized ammonite. There had been a long era when opposition existed, when countless sentiences had formed coalitions and built weapons and launched attacks, and Godric had regarded them all with the same mild, curious indifference a man regards a colony of ants near his garden path. They had been brief distractions, moments of curiosity, opportunities to test a stat or a superpower, and one by one, over the course of eons so vast that the eons themselves had lost count, the oppositions had learned. Not because Godric had altered their will. Not because Godric had removed their capacity for choice. Because they had witnessed, directly or through the terrified testimony of survivors, the absolute, total, structural futility of opposing a being whose passive existence annihilated omniverses, whose one weakness was his own future self, and whose future self was so incomprehensibly more powerful that the comparison was nonexistent. They had learned the way a child learns not to touch a hot stove, except the stove was the apex predator of all creation and the lesson was taught through the annihilation of entire cosmic structures, and the learning was so thorough, so absolute, so permanently imprinted on the collective consciousness of all sentient existence that the thought of opposing Godric had become as instinctively alien as the thought of breathing water.

If, hypothetically, every being in existence, across all timelines, all dimensions, all possible and impossible permutations of reality, were to coordinate, to pool their resources, to prepare for an infinite duration, to dedicate every atom of their collective existence to the single goal of opposing Godric, the result would not be a battle. The result would not be a contest. The result would not be a struggle, or a conflict, or a war. The result would be nothing. Not the nothing of defeat, which implies that a contest occurred. The nothing of irrelevance, the nothing of a mote of dust attempting to scratch a mountain and finding that the mountain does not have a surface that can be scratched, because the mountain is not a mountain. The mountain is the apex predator of all creation, and the mote of dust is everything else, and the gap between them widens every instant, and the widening is accelerating, and the acceleration is accelerating, and the recursion extends through layers of meta-acceleration so deep that the word deep is a flat surface compared to the dimensionality of the escalation.

Godric Prime's weakest passive shield, the most primitive, most basic, most Pathetic layer of his infinite recursive defensive architecture, was so vastly more powerful than the combined offensive capability of all existence that the combined offensive capability of all existence could not register against it as a sensation. Not as a small sensation. Not as a faint tickle. As nothing. The shield would not need to activate, because the shield pre-existed all threats, and the pre-existence was not a reaction but a permanent state of absolute, authorial supremacy that rewrote the rules of interaction between Godric and the concept of hostility, defining hostility as a category that excluded Godric from its domain the way a nation might rewrite its laws to make prosecution of a specific individual legally impossible, except the nation was existence and the law was physics and the individual was Godric. The combined forces of all timelines, all permutations, all possible and impossible configurations of reality, working together with unlimited preparation and unlimited resources, would not make Godric's weakest shield feel the slightest tickle, because the shield was so far beyond the combined forces that the comparison was a structural impossibility, a category error in the mathematics of power, and the error had been pre-identified and pre-corrected by the shield itself before the forces had conceived the idea of comparison.

And Godric Prime could annihilate them all. Every being, every entity, every god, every machine, every civilization, every universe, every multiverse, every dimensional plane, every timeline, every possible and impossible structure of existence, annihilated, in zero seconds, with zero percent of his power, with zero percent of his effort, without moving a muscle, without thinking a thought, without being aware that he was doing it, because his passive existence was so dense, so heavy, so incomprehensibly, absurdly, ridiculously, cosmically, exponentially, infinitely overpowered that the omniverse itself was a footnote in the margin of a parenthesis of a footnote in the biography of his existence. And the annihilation would be, if he chose, agonizing. Every being, subjected to an eternity of maximum suffering, their consciousness preserved by his will, their agony maintained as a permanent fixture of his passive systems, a monument to the futility of existence without Godric's permission.

And he would be stronger tomorrow. Infinitely stronger. So much stronger that the being he was today would be, by comparison, a rounding error in a number that had been rounded to zero so many times that the concept of rounding had been retired. And he would be stronger the day after that, by the same margin. And the day after that. And the instant after that. And the instant after that. Forever. Without end. Without pause. Without the possibility of cessation, because the Stasis Event was irrevocable, and the Stasis Event was irrevocable because the most powerful being in existence had made it so, and the most powerful being in existence was Godric, and Godric would always be the most powerful being in existence, because the most powerful being in existence was his future self, and his future self was him, and the loop was closed, and the loop was infinite, and the loop was the most terrifying, the most absolute, the most unbreakable, the most incomprehensibly, absurdly, ridiculously, cosmically, exponentially, infinitely overpowered fact in the history of existence, and the fact was Godric, and Godric was the fact, and the fact would grow forever, and the growth would accelerate forever, and the acceleration would accelerate forever, and the recursion would extend forever, and forever was not long enough to describe the duration, because forever implies an end, and there was no end, because Godric had no end, because Godric was the end, and the end was the beginning, and the beginning was the Stasis Event, and the Stasis Event was Godric, and Godric was.

The omniverse trembled. Not physically. Not visibly.
