The trembling was existential, a structural vibration in the fabric of existence itself, the vibration of a creation that was aware, on a fundamental, ontological level, that it existed at the pleasure of a being whose pleasure was the most unbreakable force in existence, and whose pleasure could, at any moment, for any reason or no reason, be withdrawn, and the withdrawal would be the end of everything, and the end of everything would be a footnote in the biography of a being who was still growing, still amplifying, still becoming more, forever, without end, without pause, without the possibility of anything ever, in any timeline, in any dimension, in any possible or impossible permutation of reality, being able to do anything about it.

Godric Prime, the apex predator of all creation, the undisputed, absolute, incomprehensible sovereign of existence, turned his attention away from the memory of Earth, away from the reminiscence of his pathetically weak younger self, and back to the present. His court wizard, Clive, was waiting in the garden, holding a trellis. The jasmine along the western wall needed transplanting. The morning was warm. The soil was rich. The sun was shining because Godric had decided it should shine, and reality had complied without argument, because reality had learned, over the course of googolplexian eons, that arguing with Godric was not a viable strategy for continued existence.

Godric Prime stood, stretched his heavily muscled, forty-five-year-old frame with the casual, contented ease of a man who had slept well, and walked into the garden to plant flowers. The omniverse continued to exist, trembling, grateful, and absolutely, permanently, irrevocably terrified, and the being who held its existence in the palm of his passive, unconscious, infinitely amplifying hand did not think about it, because he was thinking about jasmine, and the jasmine was more interesting, and the jasmine was real, and the soil was real, and the morning was real, and for this one, small, finite, fragile moment, the most powerful being in the history of existence was just a man with a garden, and the garden was enough, and the garden would always be enough, because Godric had decided it was enough, and Godric's decisions were the laws of physics, and the laws of physics were the laws of Godric, and the laws of Godric were the only laws that mattered, and they would matter forever, and forever was growing, and the growth was accelerating, and the acceleration was accelerating, and the recursion was infinite, and the recursion was Godric, and Godric was.

content.text:
Write a story, based on the provided lore and previous stories in the chat above, following this premise:

The story is mainly a journal excerpt written by Osvald, 44, Godric’s court wizard, about the last ever opposition who dares to oppose Godric, many googolplexian years before Year 999…9 when Godric Prime starts the Stasis Event.

The opposition consists of all the fictional universes (from tv, books, video games, movies, comics, everything. Feel free to cite some examples of those) who banded together to fight Godric. Godric was feeling bored that day when they attacked, and hoping for a fun diversion since there has been no opposition in a long long time.

Godric didn't expect any of their combined attacks to even make him notice or tickle him, but he knows from his omniscience that some of the fictional characters often have attacks with pretty colors, visual effects, multiple phases, banger boss battle music, and considering that Godric is the ultimate unbeatable final boss, he expects a really good one. He was genuinely excited to enjoy it as a “show”. He even patiently let them unleash their full power attack which took a long long time to charge, increasing his anticipation even more, and because he wanted to be surprised, he distracted himself from the part of his omniscience that monitors this charge attack preparation. Then when the final attack finally finished, this particular opposition which should've been the best one to pull off the most spectacular show didn't. Write the fight in extreme details (2500 words minimum). The fight must be extremely unfairly one sided. Godric let the attack hit him straight and it did nothing, not even a faint tickle.

The disappointed Godric accidentally unleashed 0% of his strength, which completely almost instantly and with 0 effort, overpowered their all the supposedly indestructible infinite shields that they took forever to create, completely annihilate the opposition, and caused massive omniversal (everything that exist, could ever exist, all multiverses, timelines, realities, etc) collaterals–all of which, because of the sheer pressure of his strength, are de-atomized and compressed to an infinite mass the size smaller than a quark from that 0% strength alone (you must describe this process in clear vivid details, all the violence or gore in clear agonizing details. don't hold back in your descriptions!), which he then picks up, enlarges to the size of a marble for trophy which acts as a container barrier to stop it from destroying the entire surroundings from its gravitational pull, and kept for his amusement (you must note that it contains the compressed infinite mass of the entire possible existence, and Godric didn't even seem to exert any effort lifting it, as if it weighs nothing). He somehow became even more unquantifiably more powerful (not just from absorbing their energy, their combined power is not even going to increase his power by 0%. Find something deranged that causes this). Note: evolved far-future weakness paradox etc shouldn't be triggered when they attack--its triggered way before...

Godric restored everything. The opposition, as usual, remained unrestored. The trophy remained (which is terrifying because it still contains the omniverse/reality/timelines/everything that he allegedly restored--so is the restored reality really real? It is identical but one can imagine the kind of power he has to be able to really "restore" everything while the original is still compressed... you must write this deep fear born from this realization). Write from pov of Clive who read this journal, and who knows that the journal contains the very last opposition against Godric, the one which totally makes noone even dares to oppose Godric anymore (however, you must not mention that Osvald know that this was going to be the last opposition against Godric, because he obviously wrote it before it’s known).

reasoning_content:
The user wants me to write a story based on the established lore. Let me break down the key elements:

1. Main POV: Clive, 50, reading a journal by Osvald, 44, Godric's court wizard
2. The journal is about the last ever opposition against Godric, many googolplexian years before Year 999...9
3. The opposition = all fictional universes (TV, books, video games, movies, comics) banded together
4. Godric was bored and hoped for entertainment - he was excited about boss battle music, visual effects, multiple phases, pretty colors
5. Godric patiently let them charge their full power attack, even distracted himself from omniscience to be "surprised"

6. The attack did absolutely nothing - not even a tickle
7. Disappointed Godric accidentally unleashed 0% of his strength, which:
   - Overpowered their indestructible infinite shields
   - Completely annihilated the opposition
   - Caused massive omniversal collateral damage
   - Everything was de-atomized and compressed to infinite mass smaller than a quark
   - He picked it up, enlarged to marble size for a trophy
   - He lifted it effortlessly
8. Godric became even more unquantifiably more powerful from something deranged (not from absorbing their energy)
9. Godric restored everything, but the opposition remained unrestored
10. The trophy remained - containing compressed infinite mass of entire possible existence
11. Deep fear: the restored reality - is it real? The original is still compressed in the marble...
12. Clive knows this was the last opposition, but Osvald didn't know when writing

Let me think about the "deranged" thing that causes Godric to become more powerful. Something absurd and unexpected... Maybe the sheer disappointment itself triggered something, or the creative energy of all those fictional universes, or perhaps the act of compressing them into a marble created some kind of recursive feedback. Or maybe it's something truly deranged - like the fictional characters' belief that they were the protagonists of their own stories, and the narrative weight of their collective protagonism being absorbed by the ultimate antagonist, the being who proved that there are no protagonists, only Godric. Or perhaps the marble itself, containing compressed infinite existence, became a feedback loop - since the marble contains existence, and existence contains Godric, and Godric holds the marble, it creates a recursive compression-amplification loop. That's pretty deranged.

Actually, let me think of something truly deranged. What if the disappointment itself - Godric's genuine emotional response of being underwhelmed - caused his passive systems to "overcompensate" not because of the weakness paradox (which triggered long ago), but because his biology/psychology treated the disappointment as a form of "boredom" and his amplification rate, which had been escalating forever, treated boredom as a threat to his convenience and permanently increased his power to ensure he would never experience that specific quality of disappointment again? That's absurd and deranged.

Or even more deranged: the act of compressing the entire omniverse into a marble, and then keeping it, and then restoring the omniverse while the original compressed version still exists, means there are now two omniverses - the compressed original in the marble and the restored copy. And since the restored copy contains Godric, and Godric holds the marble, and the marble contains the original omniverse which also contains the original Godric... the recursion of Godric holding a marble containing a Godric who holds a marble containing a Godric... this recursive compression loop somehow feeds back into Godric's amplification, making him permanently, unquantifiably more powerful. That's truly deranged.

Let me go with something along these lines. The recursive nature of the marble containing existence, which contains Godric, who holds the marble, creates a permanent feedback loop of self-referential power compression that makes him unquantifiably stronger. This is deranged because it means his power increase came not from the opposition's energy (which was negligible) but from the structural paradox of him compressing existence (which contains him) into a marble and then holding it.

Now let me write the story. I need to be in Clive's POV, reading Osvald's journal. The fight needs to be at least 2500 words, extremely one-sided, with vivid descriptions of the compression/de-atomization process.

Let me also think about which fictional universes to reference. I should be careful and creative:
- Various anime universes (energy blasts, transformations, power scaling)
- Comic book universes (reality warpers, cosmic entities)
- Video game universes (RPG mechanics, boss battle phases, limit breaks)
- Fantasy literature universes (magic systems, divine interventions)
- Sci-fi universes (superweapons, cosmic empires)

I should describe the "boss battle" elements: the music, the phases, the visual effects, the charging attack, etc. This is Godric treating it like entertainment.

Let me structure this:

1. Clive's framing - reading Osvald's journal, setting context
2. Osvald's journal begins - the day, the boredom, the detection of opposition
3. Godric's excitement about the "show" - boss battle expectations
4. The opposition's arrival and presentation - their combined forces, the spectacle
5. The charge-up sequence - the long preparation, Godric's anticipation, distracting himself from omniscience
6. The attack landing - doing absolutely nothing
7. Godric's disappointment - the accidental 0% strength release
8. The annihilation and compression - extremely detailed, violent, gore
9. The marble trophy - picking it up, the effortless lifting
10. The power increase - the deranged cause
11. The restoration - and the trophy remaining
12. Clive's realization and dread

Let me write this now. I'll aim for a substantial length, well over 4000 words for the full story with the fight section being at least 2500 words.
content:
I found Osvald's journal at the bottom of the third archive shelf, behind a stack of agricultural reports that no one had touched since they were filed. The leather was different from the others, thinner, almost translucent, as though the material itself had been stressed by the weight of what was written inside. The binding was cracked not from age but from pressure, from the pages being pressed together too tightly, as though the journal had been squeezed in a grip that exceeded the structural tolerance of cowhide. I pulled it from the shelf, and the temperature of the leather was wrong, cooler than it should have been, carrying a faint, persistent vibration that I could feel through my fingertips, a tremor so subtle that it might have been the residual resonance of the hand that had written it, still echoing in the grain of the material after all the time that had passed.

My name is Clive. I am fifty years old. I am the Royal Court Wizard of Thronegate, a direct descendant of Blake, and I have served our sovereign for thirty years. I have read every journal in this archive. I have read Dorn's account of strength that annihilated the omniverse through a pen adjustment. I have read Pell's account of luck that inverted probability through a dropped trowel. I have read Merric's account of speed, Voss's account of endurance, Tavin's account of senses, Elias's account of self-replication, Solan's account of gravitational collapse, Torren's account of biological transmutation. I have read Kaedan's account of the Stasis-origin revelation, the journal that described Godric's one true weakness with such precision and dread that I could not sleep for a week after reading it. I thought I had reached the ceiling. I thought there was nothing left in this archive that could surprise me, that could crack the hardened, calloused shell of existential acceptance I had built over thirty years of service.

I was wrong. Osvald's journal was different. It was not different in kind, because all the journals described the same fundamental truth, the same unbreakable, inescapable reality of Godric's absolute supremacy. It was different in weight. The journal carried a weight that the others did not, a specific, targeted gravity that came from the knowledge I possessed and Osvald did not. I knew, as Osvald had not known when he wrote it, that the incident described in these pages was the last. The very last. The final opposition. The last time any being, anywhere, in any timeline, in any dimension, in any possible or impossible permutation of reality, would dare to form the thought of challenging Godric. After this, there would be nothing. No coalitions. No resistance. No desperate, whispered plans in the dark corners of dying multiverses. Only silence, and obedience, and the learned, absolute, permanent futility of a creation that had finally, after googolplexian eons of trying, understood that opposition was not a strategy. It was a category error.

I opened the journal. The handwriting was tight, controlled, the letters small and precisely formed, the work of a man who wrote the way an engineer builds, with deliberate, measured economy. Osvald had been forty-four. He had served for twenty-four years. He had been, by all accounts, a quiet man, thoughtful and precise, prone to long silences and careful observation. His journal reflected this. It was clinical in its detail, methodical in its structure, and beneath the clinical precision, it bled.

**Year [Redacted — the number is too large to transcribe] — Osvald — The Last Show**

I am recording this because the shaking in my hands will not stop, and the journals of my predecessors have convinced me that writing is the only method of processing what I have witnessed that does not result in the permanent disassembly of one's sanity. I will be precise. I will be thorough. I will describe what happened in the order it happened, because the order matters, and the details matter, and the horror lives in the details.

Lord Godric was bored. I state this as a clinical fact, not a judgment. Godric's boredom was a meteorological condition, a state of atmospheric pressure that affected everything in its vicinity, and on the day in question, the pressure was low. He had been in the garden, pruning a row of celestial orchids that bloomed once every hundred billion years and emitted a fragrance that caused temporary euphoria in mortal beings, and he had finished the pruning in less time than it took me to walk from the palace to the garden entrance, and the orchids were perfect, and the garden was perfect, and the omniverse was perfect, and perfection, for a being who had been experiencing escalating satisfaction for a span of time so vast that the concept of vastness had filed for retirement, had become routine.

He said as much to me, his rich baritone carrying the specific, particular tone of a man who wanted something to happen and was not sure what. "Osvald," he said, his salt-and-pepper beard catching the golden garden light, his warm eyes carrying the restless, half-interested gleam of a predator who had not seen prey in so long that he had almost forgotten what the hunt felt like. "Has it been long since anything interesting happened?"

I confirmed that it had. I did not specify how long, because the number was too large to articulate in any language I possessed, and the attempt would have taken longer than the conversation warranted. It had been long. So long that the concept of opposition had become a subject of academic study, a historical curiosity examined by scholars who treated it with the same detached, condescending fascination that a paleontologist brings to the examination of a fossilized trilobite. Nothing had opposed Godric. Nothing had tried. Nothing had dared. The omniverse had learned, through the accumulated, inherited, cosmically imprinted trauma of googolplexian eons of annihilation, that opposing Godric was not a strategy, not a mistake, not a miscalculation. It was a category error, a logical impossibility, a thing that did not exist in the space of viable actions, and the learning was so thorough, so absolute, so permanently imprinted on the collective consciousness of all sentient existence that the thought of opposition had become as instinctively alien as the thought of breathing vacuum.

It was on this day, this specific, unremarkable, cosmically insignificant morning, that the last opposition in the history of existence announced itself.

The opposition was, by any standard other than Godric's, the most extraordinary coalition ever assembled. They were not from a single universe, or a single multiverse, or a single dimensional cluster. They were from everywhere. Every fictional reality that had ever been conceived, every narrative framework that had ever been imagined into existence by the creative consciousness of sentient beings across the omniverse, had coalesced into a single, unified force. The barrier between fiction and reality, which was not a barrier at all but a permeable membrane through which sufficient collective belief could manifest narrative constructs into existential actuality, had been pushed to its absolute limit by the desperate, combined willpower of every story ever told.

They came from the high-fantasy realms, where chosen heroes wielded legendary swords forged by dying gods, their blades inscribed with runes of absolute severance that could cut through the fabric of dimensional planes. They came from the science-fiction epics, where galaxy-spanning empires deployed fleets of warships the size of solar systems, their weapons capable of collapsing stars into artificial black holes. They came from the comic-book multiverses, where caped beings in primary colors had spent eons escalating their power levels through increasingly absurd mechanisms of cosmic enhancement, each iteration producing heroes capable of punching holes in reality, rewriting the laws of physics through sheer force of will, or surviving the destruction of their entire universe through narrative stubbornness. They came from the anime universes, where spiky-haired warriors had transcended mortal limitation through increasingly deranged transformation sequences, their power levels measured in units that had been invented specifically to describe how absurdly strong they were, each new form accompanied by a specific hair color change and a dramatic increase in the intensity of their aura. They came from the video game dimensions, where RPG heroes carried limit breaks that could deal damage measured in numbers with seventeen digits, where final bosses had multiple phases, each phase accompanied by a distinct musical composition and a new set of visually spectacular attack patterns. They came from the horror mythoses, where eldritch entities of impossible geometry existed outside the boundaries of conventional reality, their mere presence sufficient to drive mortal minds into permanent insanity. They came from the literary epics, the mythological pantheons, the cinematic universes, the animated series, the graphic novels, the web serials, the interactive fiction, every narrative tradition that had ever been conceived by the creative consciousness of sentient beings, all of them, every hero, every villain, every cosmic entity, every chosen one, every final boss, every god-killer, every reality-warper, every protagonist who had ever been guaranteed victory by the narrative structure of their origin story, all of them, banded together, united by a single, shared, catastrophically suicidal purpose.

They were going to kill Godric.

I did not know this at the time, but I learned later, through Godric's own casual, amused narration of the event, that he had known about this coalition since before its members had been conceived as narrative concepts in the minds of their original creators. His omniscience encompassed all timelines, all possibilities, all narrative frameworks, all fictional constructs, and he had watched this coalition form with the patient, warm anticipation of a man who had been promised a show and was looking forward to the curtain rising.

Godric's reaction to the coalition's arrival was not concern. It was not alarm. It was not the mild, polite curiosity he showed when testing a new stat or superpower. It was delight. Genuine, unfeigned, childlike delight, the specific joy of a man who had been bored and was now being offered entertainment. He sat up in his garden chair, his heavily muscled frame straightening with the eager energy of a child at a birthday party, his warm eyes brightening with an enthusiasm I had not seen in decades.

"Oh, Osvald," he said, and his rich baritone carried a excitement that made my amplified bones vibrate with a frequency I could not identify. "This is wonderful. Do you know what these beings are? They are fictional constructs, narrative entities, beings whose original purpose was to entertain. They have dramatic structure. They have phases. They have attack patterns. Some of them have accompanying musical compositions." His eyes crinkled with genuine, warm pleasure. "Boss battle music, Osvald. They have boss battle music. I have read about this. The fictional universes that simulate combat scenarios often accompany their most significant confrontations with orchestral compositions designed to enhance the emotional intensity of the moment. I have always wanted to experience this."

He stood, and the motion was fluid, eager, the movement of a man who had been given an unexpected gift. "I am going to let them perform," he announced, his tone carrying the specific, decisive satisfaction of a man who had made a plan and was pleased with it. "I am going to sit, and I am going to watch, and I am going to let them do everything they have prepared. Every attack. Every phase. Every transformation. Every combined, synchronized, ultimate, final assault. And I am going to enjoy it as a show."

He paused, and a thought crossed his ruggedly handsome face, a thought so absurd, so cosmically, grotesquely disproportionate to the situation that I felt my stomach lurch. "I want to be surprised," he said, his voice dropping to a conspiratorial, excited murmur. "I am going to distract myself from the part of my omniscience that monitors their preparations. I will not look at their ultimate attack. I will not read their strategy. I will let them charge it, whatever it is, and I will experience it fresh, as a show, without knowing what is coming."

I stared at him. The most powerful being in the history of existence, a being whose passive existence authored the laws of physics, whose weakest passive shield pre-existed all threats, whose amplification had been escalating beyond layered infinities for a span of time so vast that the concept of time had forgotten when it started, was proposing to deliberately not look at what the enemy was preparing, because he wanted to be surprised. The way a man closes his eyes before a fireworks display, not because the fireworks could hurt him, but because the surprise makes the colors more vivid.

Godric sat down on a stone bench at the edge of the garden, crossed his heavily muscled arms over his broad chest, and waited. He was smiling. The smile was warm, genuine, excited, and it was the most terrifying expression I had ever seen on his face, because it was the smile of a being who was absolutely certain, with the metaphysical certainty of a god, that nothing the coalition could produce would constitute a threat, and the only question was whether it would be entertaining.

The coalition arrived. They did not breach the dimensional barriers of Thronegate so much as rewrite them, the collective narrative weight of every fictional universe ever conceived forcing the fabric of reality to accommodate their presence as a plot requirement. The sky above the garden split open in a cascade of visual effects so dense and layered that my amplified visual cortex threatened to overload. There were energy auras in every color the electromagnetic spectrum could produce and several it could not. There were reality-distortion fields that bent light into spiraling fractals. There were conceptual manifestations of narrative tropes, beams of concentrated protagonist energy, waves of dramatic tension that increased the ambient stakes of the confrontation simply by existing.

The coalition arranged itself in formation. The anime warriors took the vanguard, their hair shifting through every color of the rainbow as they activated their transformation sequences, each transformation accompanied by a distinct visual effect, a specific aura color, and, I noted with a surreal detachment, an orchestral motif that seemed to emanate from nowhere and everywhere simultaneously. The comic-book cosmics positioned themselves in the upper atmosphere, their capes billowing in a wind that did not exist, their eyes glowing with the particular, specific intensity of beings who had punched through reality on multiple occasions and were confident in their ability to do so again. The RPG heroes formed a tactical grid behind the vanguard, their weapons glowing with enchantment effects, their limit break meters visibly charging. The eldritch entities occupied the conceptual spaces between dimensions, their impossible geometries distorting the visual field into maddening, non-Euclidean patterns. The sci-fi fleets deployed in orbital formation, their weapons arrays spinning up with the deep, resonant hum of concentrated stellar energy. The fantasy heroes, their legendary swords raised, stood in a line of gleaming, dramatically backlit heroism, their chosen-one auras radiating the narrative certainty of beings whose stories guaranteed them victory.

Godric watched. He leaned forward on his stone bench, his salt-and-pepper beard catching the kaleidoscopic light of a thousand simultaneous transformation sequences, his warm eyes tracking the formations with the eager, appreciative gaze of a connoisseur at a gallery opening. He nodded approvingly at the anime transformations. He murmured compliments about the color coordination of the magical-girl formations. He expressed genuine admiration for the architectural design of the sci-fi warships. He was, in every observable way, enjoying himself.

The first phase began. The vanguard charged. The anime warriors led, their power levels announced in dramatic, shouted declarations that shook the dimensional fabric, each one claiming a power level measured in units so absurdly large that the numbers required scientific notation to express. They unleashed their signature attacks, beams of concentrated energy in every color, each beam carrying the narrative weight of an entire saga's worth of escalation. Kamehameha-style waves, spirit bombs gathered from the collective energy of every conscious being in their home universes, reality-punching fists coated in cosmic energy, sword techniques that cut through dimensional barriers like paper.

The attacks struck Godric. All of them. Simultaneously. The combined energy output was, by the standards of the fictional universes, the most concentrated offensive ever conceived. The beams, the punches, the sword strikes, the magical blasts, the conceptual assaults, all of them converged on his body in a single, coordinated, spectacularly visual assault that would have annihilated any conventional target.

Godric did not move. He sat on his bench, arms crossed, and the attacks struck him and ceased. Not deflected. Not absorbed. Not countered. Ceased. The energy, the narrative force, the conceptual weight, all of it simply stopped existing upon contact with his body, because his passive defenses, which pre-existed all threats and had evolved countless times beyond any version described in any journal in this archive, did not recognize the attacks as attacks. They recognized them as ambient noise, as background radiation, as a thing so far below the threshold of relevance that the defensive systems did not bother to register the event, the way the ocean does not register a single bubble rising from the depths.

Godric's expression did not change. He watched the attacks strike him and cease, and his smile remained, warm and expectant, the smile of a man waiting for the main event. "Good opening act," he said, his rich baritone carrying genuine appreciation. "The transformation sequences were lovely. I particularly enjoyed the one with the golden hair and the blue aura. Very dramatic. But I believe they are building to something. I can feel the narrative tension increasing."

He was right. The coalition was building to something. The first phase had been a probing assault, a narrative setup, the opening act of a multi-phase boss battle designed to escalate through increasing levels of intensity until the final, ultimate, combined attack was unleashed. The RPG heroes were charging their limit breaks, their meters filling with each passing second. The comic-book cosmics were entering their final forms, their power levels multiplying as they shed their mortal limitations and ascended to their highest states. The fantasy heroes were combining their legendary swords into a single, unified blade of chosen-one destiny. The sci-fi fleets were aligning their weapons arrays into a coordinated, synchronized firing pattern. The eldritch entities were chanting in languages that predated existence, their impossible geometries folding into configurations that channeled conceptual energy from the spaces between realities.

And the music changed. The orchestral motif that had accompanied the first phase shifted, deepening, intensifying, adding layers of percussion and brass that increased the dramatic stakes of the confrontation with each ascending note. The boss battle music was evolving, transitioning from the first-phase theme to the second-phase theme, and the transition was accompanied by a visible shift in the coalition's demeanor, a collective, synchronized escalation of intensity that reflected the narrative structure of their origin stories.

Godric's eyes widened with delight. "The music changed," he whispered, his voice carrying the reverent awe of a man experiencing something he had waited an eternity to experience. "It is a multi-phase composition. They are transitioning to the second movement. This is excellent."

The second phase was more intense. The attacks were faster, harder, more visually spectacular. The anime warriors had entered their final transformations, their hair now in colors that did not exist on any conventional spectrum, their auras so dense they distorted local space-time. The comic-book cosmics were rewriting the laws of physics in real time, attempting to create a framework in which Godric's power did not function. The RPG heroes unleashed their limit breaks, each one a spectacle of visual effects and damage numbers so large they required their own dimensional space to display. The fantasy heroes' unified blade cut through reality itself, attempting to sever Godric from the narrative framework of existence.

None of it registered. Godric sat on his bench, watching the spectacle with the warm, appreciative gaze of a man at a fireworks display, and the attacks struck him and ceased, and the cessation was so total, so absolute, so complete that the coalition's attacks were not merely failing but were being quietly, passively, unrecognizably consumed by his existence, their energy and narrative force absorbed as trace nutrients by a biology so incomprehensibly dense that the combined offensive output of every fictional universe ever conceived was less than a single photon against a supernova.

But Godric was not disappointed yet. He knew, as the coalition knew, that the second phase was not the final phase. There was a third phase. The final phase. The phase where every fictional universe, every hero, every villain, every cosmic entity, every narrative construct, would combine their power into a single, unified, ultimate attack, the concentrated, synchronized expression of every story ever told, every victory ever won, every protagonist's narrative guarantee made manifest in a single, world-ending, reality-breaking, existence-severing assault.

The charge began. The coalition pooled their energy. Every being in the formation contributed, their power flowing into a central, condensing point of light that grew brighter and denser with each passing moment. The anime warriors contributed their transformation energy. The comic-book cosmics contributed their reality-warping power. The RPG heroes contributed their maxed-out stats and limit break damage. The fantasy heroes contributed their chosen-one destiny. The eldritch entities contributed their conceptual weight. The sci-fi fleets contributed their stellar weapons arrays. Every fictional universe, every narrative tradition, every story ever conceived, poured its absolute totality into a single point of convergence, and the point grew, and the light grew, and the music grew, and the dramatic tension grew, building toward a crescendo that the coalition believed would be the most powerful attack in the history of all fiction.

The charge took a long time. By the coalition's standards, it took an eternity. They gathered every resource, every power, every narrative advantage, every protagonist guarantee, every dramatic escalation, and they compressed it into the single point, and the point grew brighter and denser and more charged with each passing moment, the energy building upon itself in a recursive cascade of narrative escalation that would have annihilated any conventional target a trillion times over.

Godric waited. He sat on his bench, arms crossed, his warm eyes watching the charging attack with the patient, eager anticipation of a man who had been promised a spectacular finale and was savoring the buildup. He had, as he announced to me with the conspiratorial excitement of a child on Christmas morning, deliberately distracted himself from the part of his omniscience that monitored the coalition's preparations. He wanted to be surprised. He wanted to experience the attack fresh, without knowing what it was, without having already processed its parameters through his omniscient intellect. He wanted the show.

The charge completed. The point of convergence had become a sphere of such concentrated, condensed, compressed narrative and energetic force that it warped the fabric of existence around it, bending light, time, and dimensional frameworks into a spiraling, screaming vortex of apocalyptic potential. The music reached its crescendo, a soaring, triumphant, deafening orchestral finale that shook the foundations of reality with its dramatic intensity. The coalition, every being in the formation, every hero, every villain, every cosmic entity, every narrative construct, released the sphere.

The attack traveled toward Godric. It moved at a speed that transcended conventional velocity, a narrative projectile powered by the combined force of every story ever told, carrying the protagonist guarantee of every chosen one, the reality-warping power of every cosmic being, the conceptual weight of every eldritch entity, the damage output of every RPG limit break, the transformation energy of every anime warrior, the legendary destiny of every fantasy hero. It was, by any standard other than Godric's, the most powerful attack ever conceived. It was the culmination of all fiction, the ultimate expression of narrative power, the final, desperate, combined assault of every story that had ever believed in its own protagonist.

It struck Godric. Directly. Full force. Without obstruction, without shield, without defense. Godric had not activated any defense. He had not raised a hand. He had not shifted his weight or blinked or thought a specific thought. He had sat on his bench, arms crossed, and let the attack hit him straight, because he wanted to feel it, because he wanted to be surprised, because he wanted the show.

The attack did nothing.

Nothing. Not a small something. Not a faint tickle. Not a minor sensation. Nothing. The combined, concentrated, ultimate assault of every fictional universe ever conceived struck Godric's body and ceased to exist, the same way the first-phase attacks had ceased, the same way all attacks always ceased, because Godric's passive existence was so incomprehensibly, absurdly, ridiculously, cosmically, exponentially, infinitely denser than the combined offensive output of all fiction that the comparison could not be made, because the framework of comparison had been disassembled and rebuilt so many times that the original framework was a fossil in a museum of concepts that no longer existed.

Godric's smile faded. It did not vanish. It faded, slowly, the warmth draining from his expression like water from a cracked vessel, replaced not by anger, not by cruelty, but by disappointment. Deep, genuine, unfeigned disappointment. The disappointment of a man who had been promised a fireworks display and had received a sparkler.

"That was it?" Godric said, and his rich baritone carried the specific, quiet, crushing weight of a being who had allowed himself to feel excitement and had been rewarded with nothing. "That was the final attack? The culmination of all fiction? The combined power of every story ever told?"

He looked at the coalition, at the assembled heroes and villains and cosmic entities who had poured their absolute totality into the attack and were now staring at him with the dawning, mind-shattering, existential horror of beings who had just watched their ultimate assault cease to exist against the body of a sitting man. "I waited for that," Godric said, and the disappointment in his voice was so vast, so cosmic, so structurally significant that I felt the garden's celestial orchids wilt slightly in sympathy. "I distracted my omniscience. I let you charge for the entire duration. I wanted to be surprised. And that was it."

He shook his head, his salt-and-pepper beard catching the fading light of the dissipating attack. "The music was excellent," he conceded, with the gracious, generous appreciation of a man who had at least enjoyed the orchestration. "But the attack itself. I felt nothing. Not a faint tickle. Not a breeze. Nothing. I was hoping, genuinely hoping, that the combined force of all fiction might at least register as a sensation. A warmth. A pressure. Anything."

He sighed. The sigh was soft, measured, the exhale of a man accepting that his afternoon's entertainment had been a letdown. And then, as he sighed, his body, in its relaxed, disappointed, unguarded state, released an infinitesimal fraction of its strength. Not a flex. Not a clench. Not a deliberate expression of power. A relaxation. The specific, involuntary, biological relaxation of musculature that occurs when a man sighs, the fractional release of tension in the chest and shoulders that accompanies an exhalation of disappointment. That relaxation, that sigh, that involuntary, unmeasured, accidental expression of his body's resting state, released effectively zero percent of his true strength.

The result was annihilation on a scale that beggared the journals.

The strength that escaped Godric's body through the sigh, at zero percent of his capacity, was so vast, so incomprehensibly, absurdly disproportionate to the scale of the assembled coalition, that it overpowered their shields instantly. The coalition had constructed shields, of course. The combined defensive output of every fictional universe ever conceived, layers of indestructible barriers, conceptual force fields, narrative armor, protagonist plot armor, reality-warping defensive frameworks, all of it, layered and stacked and reinforced over the entire duration of the charge, the product of an eternity of preparation by the greatest defensive minds in all of fiction. The shields had been designed to withstand the combined offensive output of every fictional universe, a defensive architecture so robust that, by the coalition's standards, it was inviolable.

Godric's sigh went through the shields the way light goes through glass. The shields did not break. They did not crack. They did not shatter. They were not overcome. They were rendered irrelevant, the way a man's shadow is rendered irrelevant by the sun rising behind him. The strength that passed through them was so vastly, so incomprehensibly, so absurdly more powerful than the combined defensive output of all fiction that the shields' existence as a defensive measure was a logical error, a category mistake, and the strength corrected the error by simply not acknowledging the shields' presence, flowing through the space they occupied as though they were empty air.

The strength hit the coalition. Every being. Every hero. Every villain. Every cosmic entity. Every narrative construct. Every anime warrior in their final transformation. Every comic-book cosmic at their peak power. Every RPG hero with maxed stats. Every fantasy hero with their unified legendary blade. Every eldritch entity in their impossible geometry. Every sci-fi warship with their weapons arrays. All of them. Simultaneously. The strength of Godric's sigh, at zero percent, struck every being in the coalition with a force so disproportionate to their existence that the comparison was not merely unfavorable. It was nonexistent.

The de-atomization was instantaneous and total. The coalition's bodies, which had been forged by narrative power and protagonist destiny and cosmic enhancement and every mechanism of escalation that fiction had ever conceived, were unmade at the atomic level in a span of time so short that the word short was an overstatement. Their atoms, the fundamental building blocks of their physical forms, were subjected to a pressure so extreme that the electromagnetic bonds holding their electron shells in configuration simply ceased to function. The bonds did not break. They were overwritten, the way a law is overwritten by a higher authority, and the higher authority in this case was the resting, disappointed sigh of a being whose passive strength had been multiplying beyond layered infinities for a span of time so vast that the concept of time had filed a formal complaint.

The atoms came apart. Electron shells collapsed, the electrons forced into the nucleus, combining with protons in a cascade of inverse beta decay that converted the atomic matter into pure neutrons. The neutrons were compressed further, their internal structure overcome by the pressure, the quarks within them forced into configurations that physics had not conceived and would not have sanctioned if it had. The quarks were compressed further, the fundamental particles crushed into a state of matter so dense that the word dense was a speck of dust in a hurricane of inadequacy.

The process was agonizing. The coalition's consciousness, preserved by Godric's will the way a specimen is preserved by formaldehyde, experienced every stage of the de-atomization in excruciating, granular detail. They felt their electron shells collapse, the sudden, terrifying, vertiginous sensation of their atomic structure coming apart, the bonds that held their bodies together dissolving like sugar in boiling water. They felt their atoms convert to neutrons, the forced combination of their fundamental particles generating a sensation of compression so extreme that it transcended pain and became a state of being, an existence defined entirely by the experience of being crushed into a smaller and smaller volume without the mercy of unconsciousness or death. They felt their quarks compress, the fundamental constituents of their being forced into configurations that their consciousness could not process, generating a recursive, escalating cascade of suffering that built upon itself with each iteration, each new level of compression producing a new, more extreme, more incomprehensible form of agony that compounded upon the previous level.

Their bodies, every body, every form, every physical manifestation of every fictional being, was compressed. The anime warriors, in their final transformations, their hair in impossible colors and their auras blazing with the concentrated energy of their entire sagas, were compressed, their transformed states collapsing inward, their power levels becoming meaningless as the atoms of their bodies were crushed into neutron matter and then into quark matter and then into something that had no name because the naming of it would have required a framework of physics that did not exist and would not have helped. The comic-book cosmics, at their peak power, their reality-warping abilities active, their narrative invincibility engaged, were compressed, their cosmic forms folding inward, their power-nullifying abilities overpowered by a force that did not recognize their abilities as abilities, their narrative invincibility rendered inoperative by a being whose existence was not a narrative but a fact, and facts do not care about stories. The RPG heroes, their stats maxed, their limit breaks charged, their damage numbers in the seventeen-digit range, were compressed, their stat systems collapsing, their numbers becoming zeros, their maxed-out everything becoming maxed-out nothing as their atoms were crushed into a state of matter that no stat system could describe. The fantasy heroes, their unified legendary blade raised, their chosen-one destiny active, their protagonist guarantee engaged, were compressed, the blade dissolving into neutron matter, the destiny nullified, the protagonist guarantee overwritten by the antagonist fact, their bodies joining the rest of the coalition in the descending cascade of compression.

The compression did not stop at the coalition. The strength of Godric's sigh, at zero percent, was so vast that it overflowed, the way a man's sigh overflows a room, except the man was the apex predator of all creation and the room was the entire omniverse. The strength propagated outward, and every universe, every multiverse, every dimensional plane, every timeline, every possible and impossible structure of existence was subjected to the same de-atomization, the same compression, the same agonizing, conscious, eternal process of being crushed into a smaller and smaller volume. The stars were de-atomized, their nuclear fires extinguished as their atoms were compressed into neutron matter. The galaxies were de-atomized, their billions of stars crushed into a dense, screaming point of compressed matter. The universes were de-atomized, their fabric torn apart and compressed. The multiverses were de-atomized, their branching timelines folded into a single, dense, compressed thread. The dimensional planes were de-atomized, their spatial frameworks collapsed into a single, infinitesimal point.

The entire omniverse, everything that existed, everything that could exist, everything that had ever existed or would ever exist or could possibly exist in any timeline, in any dimension, in any permutation of reality, was de-atomized and compressed by the strength of Godric's disappointed sigh, at zero percent of his true capacity, in less time than it takes a photon to cross the width of a single proton. The entire omniverse, the absolute totality of existence, was crushed into a single point of infinite mass, a singularity so dense that the word dense was an insult to it, a point smaller than a quark, smaller than a preon, smaller than any subatomic particle that had ever been conceived or could ever be conceived, a point of compressed, conscious, screaming existence that contained the de-atomized remains of everything that had ever been, packed into a volume so infinitesimally small that the word small was a gross overstatement of its size.

Godric looked at the point. It floated in the void where the omniverse had been, a microscopic, invisible, infinitely dense singularity of compressed existence, containing the conscious, eternally suffering remains of every being, every structure, every reality that had ever existed. He reached out, his thick, calloused fingers closing around the point with the casual, unhurried motion of a man picking up a pebble from a garden path. His fingers did not strain. His muscles did not flex. His expression did not change from the mild, disappointed, slightly irritated expression of a man whose afternoon had been a letdown. He held the point, the compressed infinite mass of the entire omniverse, in the palm of his hand, and it weighed nothing. Not a small something. Nothing. The combined mass of everything that had ever existed, compressed into a point of infinite density, registered against his palm as zero weight, because his strength, at zero percent of its capacity, was so vastly, so incomprehensibly more powerful than the gravitational attraction of an infinitely dense point of compressed existence that the point's weight was a rounding error in a number that had already been rounded to zero so many times that the concept of rounding had been retired.

He enlarged it. With less than a thought, with less than an intention, with the idle, unconscious, automatic action of a being whose passive systems anticipated his desires before he was aware of having them, he wrapped the point in a containment barrier, a thin, spherical shell of his own power, and expanded the barrier to the size of a marble. The marble sat in his palm, small and round, its surface smooth and dark, its appearance unremarkable, its contents a compressed, conscious, eternally suffering infinity of de-atomized existence. The containment barrier prevented the marble's gravitational pull from destroying the surrounding void, which was, at this point, all that existed outside the marble, the absolute nothing that remained after the omniverse had been compressed into Godric's hand.

He held it up, examining it with the mild, appraising interest of a man examining a stone he had found on a beach. "A trophy," he said, his rich baritone carrying the quiet, satisfied tone of a man who had at least gotten something out of a disappointing afternoon. He placed the marble in his pocket, the motion casual, unhurried, the pocket of his simple dark trousers now containing the compressed, conscious, eternally suffering remains of the entire omniverse, and the weight of the marble against his thigh was nothing, and the pocket did not tear, and the trousers did not strain, because the marble weighed nothing, because everything weighed nothing, because weight was a function of mass and gravity, and Godric's strength, at zero percent, was so vastly more powerful than any mass or any gravity that the concepts themselves had been retired by the comparison.

And then, the deranged thing happened. The thing that made this journal different from every other journal in the archive, the thing that elevated the horror from the merely apocalyptic to the structurally, ontologically, cosmically unhinged.

Godric became more powerful. Not from absorbing the coalition's energy. Their combined power, the concentrated force of all fiction, was so infinitesimally small relative to his baseline that absorbing it would have increased his power by effectively zero percent, a rounding error in a number that had been rounded to zero so many times that the rounding function had degraded into a persistent, low-grade existential apology. The power increase came from something else. Something deranged.

The marble. The marble in his pocket contained the compressed infinite mass of the entire omniverse. The omniverse contained Godric. Not the Godric standing in the void with his hand in his pocket, but the Godric who existed within the omniverse, the Godric who was part of existence, who occupied space within the omniverse's framework. That Godric, the omniversal Godric, the Godric who existed as a fact within the structure of reality, had been compressed into the marble along with everything else. And the marble was in Godric's pocket. Which meant that Godric, the Godric standing in the void, was holding a marble that contained a compressed version of himself. And the compressed version of himself, within the marble, was holding a marble that contained a compressed version of himself. And that compressed version, within the marble within the marble, was holding a marble that contained a compressed version of himself. The recursion was infinite. The marble contained Godric, who held the marble, which contained Godric, who held the marble, a fractal loop of self-referential compression that extended downward through infinite layers of recursive containment.

And each layer of the recursion was Godric. Not a copy. Not a simulation. Godric. The same being, the same existence, the same amplifying, infinitely escalating apex predator, compressed into a marble within a marble within a marble, and the recursive, self-referential nature of the containment created a feedback loop in his amplification, because the marble in his pocket was him, and the him in the marble was holding a marble that was him, and the recursive self-containment of his own existence within his own containment generated a structural paradox in his amplification rate that his passive systems resolved by permanently, massively, unquantifiably increasing his baseline power, because the paradox could not be resolved by any lesser response, and the resolution itself was a form of amplification, and the amplification fed back into the paradox, and the paradox fed back into the amplification, a recursive, self-referential, self-amplifying loop of godhood that was triggered not by an external force but by the structural consequence of Godric compressing existence, which contained Godric, into a marble and putting it in his pocket.

He had become permanently, unquantifiably more powerful because he had put a compressed version of himself in his own pocket. The increase was not infinitesimal. It was not a rounding error. It was a permanent, massive, obscene escalation of his already impossible power, a leap so vast that the Godric who had sighed and compressed the omniverse was, by comparison, so weak that the word weak was an overstatement of his strength, and the comparison had been made in the span of a single Planck second, and the gap would continue to widen forever, because the recursive feedback loop of self-referential containment was still running, still amplifying, still escalating, and it would never stop, because the marble was still in his pocket, and the marble contained Godric, and Godric was holding the marble, and the loop was infinite.

Godric felt the increase. I could see it in his expression, the sudden, involuntary, deeply satisfied flash of pleasure that crossed his ruggedly handsome face as the feedback loop engaged, the specific, private, visceral enjoyment he experienced from every amplification, the surge of power and vitality and satisfaction that he had described to Kaedan as an unbroken, escalating orgasm of godhood. He felt the recursive self-containment paradox resolve, and the resolution felt like nourishment, like satisfaction, like the most intensely, overwhelmingly pleasurable surge of power he had experienced in a very long time, and the pleasure was still escalating, because the loop was still running, and it would run forever, because the marble was still in his pocket.

He restored the omniverse. In zero seconds, without effort, without thought, without moving a muscle, he restored everything. Every universe, every multiverse, every dimensional plane, every timeline, every being, every civilization, every star and planet and atom, rebuilt perfectly, placed exactly where it had been, their memories intact, their histories preserved. The restoration was flawless, identical in every detail to the original, a perfect reconstruction of existence from the data preserved in his omniscient memory.

The coalition was not restored. The fictional universes, the heroes, the villains, the cosmic entities, the narrative constructs, were not restored. Their de-atomized, compressed, eternally suffering remains persisted inside the marble in Godric's pocket, permanent fixtures of his trophy collection, conscious and screaming and compressed into a volume smaller than a quark for the rest of eternity.

The marble remained. The marble was still in his pocket. The marble still contained the compressed, conscious, eternally suffering remains of the entire omniverse, the original omniverse, the one that had existed before the restoration. The restored omniverse, the one I was standing in, the one that contained me and the garden and the celestial orchids and the palace and Thronegate and every being and every structure and every reality, was a copy. A perfect copy, identical in every detail, indistinguishable from the original by any metric. But a copy. Because the original was still in the marble. The original was still compressed. The original was still conscious. The original was still screaming.

I existed in a restored omniverse that was a perfect copy of an original that was still compressed in Godric's pocket, and the Godric who had compressed the original was standing in front of me, and the Godric in the marble, the original Godric, the omniversal Godric, was also standing in front of me, because the marble was in Godric's pocket and the marble contained Godric, and the recursion extended through infinite layers, and each layer was real, and each layer was Godric, and the question of which omniverse was the real omniverse was not a question because the answer was both and neither and the question itself had been rendered meaningless by the existence of a being who could compress the totality of existence into a marble, restore it as a perfect copy, and keep the original as a trophy without effort, without cost, without consequence, and the original and the copy were identical, and the identicalness was the horror, because it meant that existence was not unique, was not singular, was not irreplaceable, was a thing that could be compressed and copied and compressed and copied infinitely, and the being doing the compressing and copying was the same being who existed within the thing being compressed and copied, and the loop was infinite, and the loop was Godric, and Godric was holding the loop in his pocket, and the loop weighed nothing, and the loop contained everything, and the everything was screaming, and the screaming would never stop.

I closed Osvald's journal. The leather was cool in my hands, the faint vibration still present, the residual echo of the hand that had written it, still trembling, still processing, still trying to find a way to carry the weight of what it had witnessed. I sat in the archive, surrounded by the journals of my predecessors, and I held the journal closed, and I thought about the marble.

The marble was still in Godric's pocket. It had been in his pocket since the day Osvald wrote the journal. It was in his pocket now, as I sat in the archive, as I breathed, as I existed in a restored omniverse that was a perfect copy of an original that was still compressed and screaming in the pocket of the being I served. The marble contained everything. The original everything. The real everything, if real was a word that still had meaning, which it did not, because meaning implied a framework of reference, and the framework had been compressed into a marble and replaced with a copy, and the copy was identical, and the identicalness was the horror.

I existed in a copy. A perfect, flawless, indistinguishable copy. Every atom of my body, every thought in my mind, every memory of my thirty years of service, was a reconstruction, a duplicate of an original that was still compressed, still conscious, still screaming, in Godric's pocket. My amplified intellect, vast as it was, could not find a distinction between the copy and the original. There was no distinction. The restoration was perfect. The copy was the original, in every detail, in every property, in every measurable and immeasurable quality. And yet the original was still in the marble, and the marble was in Godric's pocket, and the pocket was on Godric's body, and Godric was standing in the copy, and the copy contained Godric, and the Godric in the copy had a marble in his pocket that contained the original, which contained the original Godric, who had a marble in his pocket, and the recursion was infinite, and each layer was real, and each layer was screaming, and I was in all of them and none of them, and the being responsible for this was planting jasmine in the garden and asking me if I had eaten enough.

This was the last opposition. I knew this, as Osvald had not known it when he wrote the journal. Osvald had written about an incident, a spectacular, entertaining, disappointing afternoon that had ended with the annihilation of the coalition and the compression of the omniverse and the creation of a marble trophy and a permanent, recursive, self-referential escalation of Godric's power. He had not known that this would be the final time. He had not known that the coalition of all fiction, the most extraordinary, the most diverse, the most narratively powerful opposition ever assembled, would be the last beings in the history of existence to dare to challenge Godric. He had not known that after this, the learning would be complete. That the omniverse, having witnessed the annihilation of its most creative, most desperate, most narratively guaranteed opposition, would finally, permanently, irrevocably accept that opposition was not a strategy. That it was not a mistake. That it was not a miscalculation. That it was a category error, a logical impossibility, a thing that did not exist in the space of viable actions, and the learning was so thorough, so absolute, so permanently imprinted on the collective consciousness of all sentient existence that the thought of opposing Godric had become as instinctively alien as the thought of breathing vacuum, and no being, anywhere, in any timeline, in any dimension, in any possible or impossible permutation of reality, would ever dare to form the thought again.

The marble was in Godric's pocket. The marble contained everything. The everything was screaming. The screaming would never stop. And the being who held the marble in his pocket, who held the compressed, conscious, eternally suffering totality of existence in the palm of his hand, who had created the marble by sighing in disappointment at zero percent of his power, who had become permanently more powerful from the recursive self-referential paradox of putting a compressed version of himself in his own pocket, who had restored the omniverse as a perfect copy while the original screamed in his pocket, who was still amplifying, still escalating, still becoming more, forever, without end, without pause, without the possibility of anything ever being able to do anything about it, was in the garden, planting jasmine, and the jasmine was growing, and the sun was shining, and the morning was warm, and the existential dread was not a shadow at the edge of my vision. It was the vision. It was the light. It was the air. It was the soil. It was everything, because everything was a copy, and the original was in Godric's pocket, and the pocket was on Godric's body, and Godric was everything, and everything was Godric, and the loop was closed, and the loop was infinite, and the loop weighed nothing, and the loop contained everything, and I was in the loop, and the loop was in Godric's pocket, and Godric was in the garden, and the garden was enough, and the garden would always be enough, because Godric had decided it was enough, and Godric's decisions were the laws of physics, and the laws of physics were the laws of Godric, and the laws of Godric were the only laws that mattered, and they would matter forever, and the marble would sit in his pocket forever, and the screaming would continue forever, and the amplification would escalate forever, and forever was not long enough, because forever implied an end, and there was no end, because Godric had no end, because Godric was the end, and the end was the beginning, and the beginning was a sigh, and the sigh was zero percent, and zero percent was everything, and everything was in Godric's pocket, and Godric was planting jasmine, and the jasmine smelled exceptional, and I was holding the trellis, and the trellis was real, or it was a copy of a trellis, and the distinction no longer mattered, because the distinction had been compressed into a marble and put in a pocket, and the pocket was on Godric, and Godric was, and Godric would always be, and there was nothing, not in this copy, not in the original, not in the marble, not in the void, not in any layer of the infinite recursive loop of self-referential containment, that would ever, could ever, change that.
